column121: Better monitor FO actions. Increase awareness of calculating descent points using distance from the runway to approximate a 3 degree descent path to the runway in order to avoid unnecessary and lengthy low level segments.  Flying at 3;000 feet about 15 miles WSW of IND direct to BIGRD on the RNAV Y 5R. Approach asked if we had the field in sight; we responded that we did; and then we were given a left turn pretty much direct to HUUPS; which is the final approach fix on the RNAV Y 5R; and cleared for the visual approach. Tower then handed us off to Tower. During this time while I was switching to Tower I became task saturated with my PM flying duties from the frequency change; getting a landing clearance; and the FO requesting flap configuration changes. After we were cleared to land I noticed we were just about to level off at 2;100 feet. Well below a 3 degree descent path given our current position to the runway. I had not noticed the FO had set 2;100 feet; which is the altitude at HUUPS; and started a descent as soon as we were cleared for the approach. We leveled off at 2;100 feet and continued for some time before reaching HUUPS and continuing the descent for the runway. We were level at approximately 1;300' feet AGL for a while before descending for landing. I don't think this was technically illegal or in violation of an FAR; as we were cleared for the visual and in the process of making a descent for landing; but I do feel it's not a good technique to simply set the FAF altitude; dive and drive to that altitude; level off for several miles; then continue descent once over the FAF. We were not on a published portion of an approach; and well below a 3 degree descent path to the runway. This wasn't an issue for this particular approach; but it could become an issue at night in mountainous terrain or if there are obstacles in the area.

column121: Inbound to ZZZZ on ZZZZZ Star.  Taken off STAR early and cleared direct to ZZZZ1 VOR.  We were instantly 1;800 feet above the path.  Proactively used speed brakes and planned to slow and extend flaps when below 20;000 feet; but were then told to keep speed at 250 KTS.  Lowered landing gear shortly after passing ZZZZ1 to get back on profile.  Was able to regain the VNAV path just prior to ZZZZZ1 and at Vref +10 per procedure.  Flaps were at 25. Frequency was very busy and sky hazy. Very late hand off to Tower.  Localizer did not correctly ID until just prior to FAF.  FO was very busy with frequency changes; configuration changes and checklist.  Tower did not clear us to land; but instructed us to continue as an aircraft was in position on our Runway; XXR. Another aircraft was holding short of XXR waiting to cross.  At 1;000 feet I was at approach speed and called 'stable;' but was distracted by no landing clearance and aircraft on runway.  Aircraft in position was cleared for immediate takeoff; we were cleared to land; and other aircraft was given clearance to cross XXR.  Departing aircraft was slow to accelerate.  Passing 500 feet FO made 'stable' call.  Shortly after we received an aural alert indicating we did not have landing flaps selected.  Flaps indicator showed flaps at 25 degrees verses the planned and briefed of 30 degrees.  I realized I had not asked for flaps 30 and was now below 400 feet. Elected not to make a configuration change to 30 degrees below 1;000 feet per procedure.  Hesitant to go-around per procedure because I wasn't sure if the crossing aircraft had not just departed the inboard parallel Runway XXL; and the late departing XXR aircraft had just rotated.  With the very hazy sky I was concerned about a conflict and /or overtaking the other aircraft during the go-around to increase speed to Vref+10 and land with flaps 25. Landing and rollout were normal.Despite adequate and recent experience into ZZZZ; these particular controllers were hard to understand.  FO had to ask for clarification a few times in the last 1;500 feet FAF. FO had just returned from 7 months of leave; was called out on reserve; and had only flown once earlier; and 4 times in the last 7 months.  I briefed the arrival and approach thoroughly and early; but it was a Max effort with the direct clearance.  I believe he got distracted from completing the checklist when the localizer did not ID until just before the FAF.  I was distracted from not calling for final flaps with the aircraft on the runway and the hazy conditions; and the late localizer ID.Add to the note of being at Vref +10 by the ZZZZZ1 fix; to be at final landing flaps. This may have helped me if I had a goal to complete that by the fix.

column121: In preparation for our arrival into DEN we briefed for the CLASH 3 RNAV arrival and the ILS to 35R as this was the expected runway from our direction of arrival. We also included in our brief the threat in DEN of runway changes and how we'd handle that especially during the descent phase on the arrival. About 20 NM from the waypoint CLASH on the arrival ATC informed us to expect runway 35L. We then re-set up for that runway and re-briefed. There was still plenty of time for this and there was little time compression. One point of confusion; however; was that the CLASH 3 has a transition for RWY 35R; but doesn't for 35L; which only has a point at which to expect vectors. We spent some time studying the arrival and approach plates to ensure that was the case to make sure we weren't missing something. I asked my F/O to double check as he was the PM and I wanted to concentrate on the descent as the PF. This has always been confusing as some arrivals and approaches have transition points and some don't. There should be some consistency. This was the beginning of some of the time compression which would later result in some workload management issues.Some point after this ATC broke us off of the arrival and instructed us to descend to 9000 feet and gave us a heading. They then assigned us to RWY 35R. We related that we'd just been assigned 35L and they reiterated we were now reassigned to 35R. This resulted in a very rushed set up for 35R as we were now pretty close in to the approach environment. I asked the F/O to set it all up so that I could continue to fly. I quickly dialed in what I thought was the 35R LOC; but it turned out not to be. I could see that he was struggling and tried to help him out. In hindsight; this was a mistake in workload management because I should have let the F/O do all of the set up. I verbalized that we didn't need to re-brief for 35R as we'd already briefed it and so had a plan. Again; in hindsight; I'm not sure this was a correct call; but we literally did not have time to brief at that point.Shortly after this we were cleared the visual approach to 35R.  We were roughly 7 miles from FRONZ and discussed not descending too quickly so as not to bust the floor of the Class B airspace. At about the same time ATC asked us to slow to approach speed for traffic ahead of us for 35R. After slowing we ended up just about 2.5 miles in trail.  At this point we were apprised of traffic for 35L and received a TCAS TA because of the assigned heading we were given to intercept the LOC for 35R. We both saw the traffic and I decided to hand fly from this point on as we had an overshooting crosswind and given our heading there would have been a good chance of an overshoot of the LOC.At FRONZ I asked for gear down and flaps 15. At about this point I noticed that neither the LOC or GS were engaging but I had the runway and the PAPI and decided to continue as we had been cleared for the visual. The FO asked if I'd like him to try and dial in the correct LOC and I said yes if he had the bandwidth to do so. Soon after we passed through 1;000 feet and we both missed the call out as I think he was still trying to dial in the LOC frequency. Also we never accomplished the Landing Checklist as we never got to final flaps. We got to 500 feet and we made those call outs. At around 400 feet AFL we received a Too Low Flaps GPWS warning and my knee jerk reaction was to call for FLAP 30 once I realized we were still at FLAPS 15. I don't know if the FO ever selected them; however. I quickly realize this was the wrong call and called for us to go around and the FO called for it at about the same time. We executed the missed approach with no issues and were vectored expeditiously for a second approach and landing without further incident.The FO is not submitting a report as far as I'm aware as we had no ATC deviations or non-compliance with FARS.
column123: This is a report regarding Flight X ZZZ-DEN regarding the changes and ATC clearances we received during our descent and approach to land in DEN.When we reviewed the flight plan in ZZZ we loaded the CLASH3 STAR with runway 35R being our selected landing runway. The flight plan had 17R; but DEN was landing North so that was the only change made to the plan on our part versus what dispatch had planned.Prior to TOD the CA fully reviewed and briefed the STAR and approach that we were expecting. We were fully prepared to execute this plan. Upon checking in with a close in DEN center controller we were advised to expect runway 35L with the EBBLR transition. There was some discussion regarding the EBBLR transition by myself and the CA as we were attempting to figure out if the transition and how the transition linked up with the approach. However; we did this again prior to TOD and were able to be prepared for the new arrival transition and approach. As we descended into Denver; we were handed off to DEN APP. This controller changed our runway back to 35R. We were well into the descent at this point and fairly close to the airport. The CA instructed me to load up the new approach and as I was attempting to do so ATC took us off the arrival for vectors and speed changes. At this point we were South of DEN but still fairly close in. I was still working on getting the FMC setup when ATC vectored us due West and then turned us on to final for 35R. The vector was vey poorly planned by the controller and both the CA and I could see that we were going to blow through the localizer if we allowed the A/P to handle the intercept. The CA disengaged the A/P and made a manual turn to join the localizer. At this point we could both see that the vector had us close in behind another airplane in final. ATC requested that we slow to 160 knots. The CA asked for me to deploy the gear and the flaps to down and 15. Shortly after ATC then requested us to slow to the slowest possible speed. Both the CA and I were closely monitoring the separation between us and the preceding aircraft. At this point I noticed there was a message on the FMC scratchpad that I had not seen before and did not recognize. I don't remember exactly what the message was but it did indicate that there was a conflict between the FMC selected approach and what the VHF radios were tuned to. I was rapidly trying to get the correct frequency on the NAV radios but my JEPP app on the iPad was not responding quickly as I was attempting to zoom in to view the frequency box. At this point we were somewhere around 1;000 feet AGL. The CA announced that he had the runway and the PAPIs in sight and was going to land and just disregard the FMC He was busy attempting to slow and again we were both concerned about the separation between us the preceding aircraft. That was what are focus was on at this point. I cannot recall if either the 1;000 ft or the 500 ft call outs were made. We were both totally task saturated at this time. At some altitude below 500 ft we received a warning message 'too low-flaps'. We both looked at the flap position and realized we were still at flaps 15. The CA called for flaps 30 and I monetarily selected flaps 30 before advocating that we needed to go around. The CA also realized we had to go around at almost the same instant I was stating that we needed to go around. So we executed the go around. We did have the 10;000 ft foot missed approach altitude selected in the MCP so we were climbing to that altitude. However; while just climbing through 7;000 the tower told us to maintain 7;000. I advised tower that we were at 7;200. Tower instructed us to maintain 7;100. We descended back to 7;100 feet and flew runway heading and completed the rest of the go around profile. Tower asked us for the reason for the GA and I replied 'configuration'. They handed us off to approach and we were vectored back around for another ILS to runway 35R. We ensured everything was properly setup and landed without further incident.Some comments about this incident. Both the CA and I had discussed the threat of Denver and how they often make changes to arrivals and approaches during cruise. In fact the CA had been reading in the flight ops newsletter on his iPad about the problems we have been having with arrivals into Denver. We were both fully aware that Denver presented a threat and thought that we had planned and were prepared for our arrival. Had we been cleared as planned per our original plan we would have been fine. Even after the first change to runway 35L we had time to plan and brief. It was only after the final switch back to 35R that we both began to become task saturated with the speed changes; the off route vectors; the FMC programming; the NAV radios; the poor vector to join final; and finally the separation between us and the preceding aircraft and the speed changes made while on final in regards to that aircraft. The amount of items needed to be accomplished while this was occurring in the terminal area was long and complex. We both became so overcome that we must have failed to accomplish the landing checklist; which would have had both of us realizing that we were not in the landing configuration.We made errors along the way; failing to get some of the call outs accomplished; FMC programming; and NAV radio frequency selection among some of the errors. However; many of these errors were driven by the nature of the changes being made by ATC in regards to arrival vectors; speed changes; runway and approach changes among other demands being made by ATC in a very time compressed period while flying within (or near) the terminal area.One more note about the runway changes in Denver. I know Denver and am aware of its threat even on departure. So while we were getting ready to depart and head to ZZZ2 I asked the ramp controller which runway was in use for the ZZZZZ SID. He stated XXR. Yet; when we received our push clearance 15-20 minutes later he told us to expect runway XY. I don't know if he was given bad information by the ATC tower or what happened; but yet again here we are in a high workload situation having to make changes to the FMC and request new TO data.Because of our short turn time in Denver the CA decided to debrief the entire arrival event into Denver while in cruise flying back to ZZZ2. We spent a good 30-40 minutes discussing the flight. Threat and errors were discussed; we used the briefing cards and identified what had occurred on the flight. I am not going to make excuses for the errors that we made; even if they occurred during a task saturated situation. However; a couple of my observations from this event were that we had talked about Denver ATC being a threat. We thought that we had addressed this threat by a good solid brief for the originally planned arrival; even after the change to the 35L arrival; we had time to plan and brief. We were ready; but ATC in Denver threw us some many changes and curve balls that we both were task saturated to the point leading to an undesired aircraft state. Fortunately; both the CA and I followed our training and SOP. safely executed a missed approach; and subsequently landed safely and uneventfully on the next approach.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported a high workload environment on approach into DEN airport; which included several runway changes; confusion over arrival procedures; and an unstable approach resulted in a go-around.

column121: On Approach into ZZZ [airport] at night on the ZZZZZ arrival requested Runway XX for landing from ZZZ Approach. ZZZ Approach then instructed a 180 heading for the Runway XX. We could see we were getting on descent that was already going to be high to start with and requested a descent from ZZZ Approach. ZZZ let us down to 5;200 feet due to terrain in the area. This was not how we had briefed or planned to enter the visual approach. We had discussed and gone over the pages enroute and decided based on weather and winds and go-around factors that the coded visual to 15 would be the wisest choice. However; we thought that would start from ZZZZZ1. The vector ATC gave us pointed the plane toward the final approach fix for the Coded Visual Approach ZZZZZ2 bypassing the rest of the waypoints on the approach. The FO was flying the approach and used a high rate of descent to get down. Got a descent rate call on the approach but that was also expected due to the 3.2 degree glide slope. At 1;000 feet AGL I was 10 knots fast right at the edge of the stable and decent rate was 1350 ft/min. I thought I had til 500 feet to get stable since it was a visual approach. I should have either gone around; requested Runway Y or maybe executed an S-Turn. The plane landed uneventfully in about 3;500 feet using a Flaps 40 landing.This was my first time flying into ZZZ and the Captain had only flown in there once before in the previous year also has not flown much during the year due to leave. ATC gave us a vector toward the Final Approach Fix on the Coded Visual Approach which was not expected from reading the pages. Also; the Captain and I were under the belief we had until 500 feet to be stable due to the old SOPs. Furthermore; on the descent into ZZZ using the ZZZZZ3 arrival you are on with ZZZ1 Approach and do not get handed off to ZZZ Approach until 6;000 feet so you don't get a runway assignment very far out. Being at night first time into a short field with mountainous terrain was a cause as well.I think on the company pages it would help to paint a better picture if there were notes to not accept a vector to the visual and proceed direct to ZZZZZ for the visual approach to Runway X if that is the runway of choice. If entering on the ZZZZZ3 arrival a note letting the crew know they won't talk to ZZZ Approach til below 10;000 feet. Perhaps at night to not accept a visual approach to Runway X and use the ILS to Runway Z.

column118: Flight Crew Became Reoriented
column121: After transitioning from an RNAV IMC approach to daytime visual references; stable; on-speed and glide-path; I briefly became distracted by an unexpected appearance of what turned-out to be a paved runway environment prior to the actual threshold. This resulted in a momentary increase in vertical speed outside stable parameters; which was corrected immediately upon verification of the PAPI and touch down zone. Limited lighting equipment in operation; and with that limited intensity of said lighting during daytime. Pavement surface area markings also difficult to discern.  The preceding aircraft made several broadcasts to the controller regarding the lighting availability and intensity. There is limited reference to a displaced threshold nor to any significant extension of such surface area published in either the 10-9 or company pages. Updating this might be considered helpful for pilots to properly frame the runway environment when transitioning from lower-visibility or lower-ceiling conditions.
column123: FO was PF. Flight and approach were normal and paced. We followed another aircraft that asked; maybe 3-4 times; if the 'lights were up' (relevant later) but that they saw the airport @ 1000'. 1000' was stable. Of note; I did not make the 500' call due to the proximity to mins but he did (possibly thinking I forgot it?). At 500' he was stable (sink 800 roughly). Minimums were set at 530' MSL (MDA + 50'; 434' AGL). Weather was worse than reported - right at minimums with rain. FO called 'runway in sight' and proceeded to push the nose over. I saw a sink rate on the VSI of approx 1350'. I immediately called it out to his attention which he then corrected; while getting a 'sink rate'. Since he was correcting and now had the (actual; more on that later) runway in sight; we proceeded to land in the touchdown zone. At the gate the review showed 1498' vertical speed(!).The night before this flight I commuted two legs and spent the night in a hotel. On my commute/hotel stay I reviewed several items and the SYCJ airport plates on Jepp FD. I even pulled up the NOTAMs for the airport so as not to be caught off guard. I cannot overstate how much prep work was done with this. No where in any document did I see information about a deconstructed runway threshold extending from R/W 06 @ SYCJ. On the JeppFD pro AMM 10-9 there is a LARGE blob of red; prior to R/W 06; but no mention what it is. There was mention of 'elevated' threshold lights and cables but nothing to indicate that a large runway threshold was prior to the R/W 06 threshold. This is important because what I believe happened is due to approaching minimums; the FO was 'antsy' to see the runway (as we all are); so he began looking outside. In his haste to 'find' the runway; he saw the deconstructed threshold (there were white X's; albeit degraded; from what I remember; as we got closer). Quite frankly; had he not called it; I most likely would have as well. Due to the weather conditions what one could make out was asphalt that looked like a runway; except we looked very high. He then pushed the nose over in an attempt to 'correct' for his perceived inaccurate vertical guidance occurring from this visual illusion which is what precipitated the increased vertical speed. R/W 06 solely has a pair of REILs and a PAPI...not your traditional ICAO lit approach so no threshold lights or a rabbit delineating the runway.The reason for submitting this report is to bring attention to the large displaced runway threshold in front of R/W 06. During visual conditions; this is immaterial; but due to the weather conditions being literally at minimums this can cause an inadvertent flight path correction. Of interest; I have found online some mention that the runway was expanded roughly 975m (3200') to 10;500. From what I can piece together it's possible the runway was expanded and now is being torn up again (or redone). So if true that (what looks like a displaced threshold) is 3000+' long. Again; in visual conditions that is not an issue...but with rain and low CIGs; it can pose a problem.

column121: Descending into ZZZ on a visual approach to Runway XXL; but following the guidance of the ILS.  A stable and uneventful approach. Upon landing we had a 6-7 knot tailwind. We landed longer than normal. I wouldn't say that we were outside of the touchdown zone; but it was very close. From my perspective the main wheels touched down prior to the end of the touchdown zone. We had floated a bit and I had to wait for the aircraft to lose that lift and start sinking into the runway before executing my landing flare. Elected not to go around and continue because nothing was unsafe we had a 10;996 foot runway and I elected to use max thrust reversers upon landing to slow us down safely.There was a few contributing factors. The main one being the tailwind on landing. Second was because of less travel due to COVID we sometimes have very light airplanes. Our landing weight was under 36;000 lbs being very light for the aircraft. So the lightweight along with the tailwind wasn't ideal. Lastly; company has recently within the last year or so updated our procedures where we add X knots to our straight in landing speeds regardless of the situation. Not having to add that extra X knots would have helped us carry less momentum into our final approach. We do bleed off the airspeed before touching down; although it's never perfect.The airport should have been landing in the other direction with the wind favoring the other runway; and it would also be more safe because of less terrain that we can't see at night. Also I should have thought about our extra light airplane and possibly requested the other runway. Also; It has been my opinion since it has started that airline go back to the normal landing numbers without adding extra 5 knots. And let the pilots decide when the extra airspeed is necessary depending on environmental factors.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported a long landing possibly outside of the landing zone.  Reporter attributed discrepancy to possible company policy of adding speed to straight in landings and the aircraft being light in weight.

Said it went off as [I] was at 600 feet 2 1/2 miles out. I should have stayed at 200-300 feet higher till a bit closer. Was on final with a very strong quartering headwind. Wanted to land under power due to strong crosswind. Maintaining 700 feet till 1 mile out was plan and Tower gave me an altitude alert. Said it went off as [I] was at 600 feet 2 1/2 miles out. I should have stayed at 200-300 feet higher till a bit closer.

column121: Landing [Runway] XXL on final; aircraft in front of us reported a 10 knot loss. The FO was pilot flying at the time and over corrected for the wind; causing us to descend below glideslope very quickly on short final.  As soon as I was calling go-around the GS aural warning came on.  The FO executed a go-around. Because of the difficult wind conditions and seeing we were already at 2;100-2;200 LBS; I decided that I would land the second attempt as I did not want to get into a fuel situation. We were in agreement with this.  On short final for the second attempt; with me as pilot flying; at about 150 feet; we had the wind-shear caution alert and proceeded to execute our second go-around. ATC gave us instructions to set up for [Runway] XXR but due to fuel concerns I said 'Unable' and declared min fuel. Also; I added that if we did have to take any delays we would be declaring emergency fuel. ATC set us up immediately for a close pattern for [Runway] XXL. On this attempt; I stayed slightly high as the wind shear seem to be the worse right at the tree line at about 150 feet.  On this third attempt; we landed successfully and taxied back to the gate.Unstable approach recognized by me and a wind shear caution message for the second attempt.Inexperienced FO who had only flown once since May 2020 and challenging weather conditions with wind shear.Two go-arounds and declared min-fuel.Have pilots who have been out a while receive more recurrent training.  It was evident due to lack of recency the FO was struggling with the conditions that day.  Also; be vigilant on [Runway] XXL as I have had issues on windy days before where the tree line ends and the field begins. With the second attempt we had a 15 knot increase with the wind shear caution message.
column123: I was the Pilot Flying. The Wind shear changes in combination with the amount of time since I've been flying in the airplane were contributing factors. There were changes of +/- 15kts and on short approach I went slightly below glide slope and then the Wind shear happened which caused the Glideslope aural warning to sound. The Captain called for a Go-Around which we executed. Once we were at a safe altitude and being vectored back for another approach the we exchanged controls and the Captain became the Pilot Flying. As we were coming in on short approach the Wind shear Aural Caution message sounded so we executed a second go-around. ATC told us that they were going to vector us around for Runway XXR; but were now low on fuel and declared min fuel. They instead kept us close pattern for Runway XXL and we landed safely. Upon landing we had 1;450 LBS of fuel. We taxied to the gate with no issues.Glideslope Aural Warning System on first attempt and Wind shear warning system on second attempt.Wind shear affecting experience level.Go-Around executed twice.Keep crews more recent on flying or more time in simulator if it's been a while.
column125: Air carrier Captain reported experiencing wind shear on final resulting in a go-around. Captain made reference to the First Officer's lack of flying as a contributing factor.

column121: Flying C172 on an IFR flight from ZZZ to ZZZ. Was flying a RNAV approach to ZZZ. Soon after hand off to ZZZ Approach; I disconnected the autopilot when the aircraft was at about 4;000 feet AGL. At that time ATC advised to turn left to 220 heading to intercept initial approach fix. Right after disconnecting the auto pilot; the aircraft banked left steeply to about 60 degrees roll and started descending while the ATC keep advising to turn left and cleared me for the RNAV approach. The conditions were IMC and aircraft was hard to control. It took a few minutes to get the aircraft under control. The ATC workload obviously increased while not knowing the exact situation and even doubted at one point if the pilot (me) was IFR rated and current. Once I was finally able to get the aircraft stabilized; I informed ATC that I was having trouble with the controls and apologized. The flight ended safely after all at ZZZ. The probable cause was having an iPad mounted on the yoke. Will never mount the iPad on the yoke again.

column121: For the ILS Runway 04R at ZZZ; performance numbers were run for a Flaps 30 landing with a good stopping margin at Autobrakes 3. ATIS winds were calling approximately 070/13 - no gusts. Upon switching to Tower at ZZZZZ on the localizer course and tracking the glideslope; Tower cleared us to land with winds now gusting to 20. As PF (Pilot Flying); I elected to add 4 knots to the Vtarget and as an added Safety measure at ZZZ; I selected Autobrakes MAX to compensate for a slightly increased approach speed. The PM (Pilot Monitoring) attempted to quickly re-run the performance data; but the gusts were not yet accounted for in the METAR. Rather than going heads down any longer inside 1;000 feet AGL; we agreed a 4-5 knot addition to VTarget and using Autobrakes MAX would be more than sufficient to account for the gust factor.At approximately 500 feet AGL; we received a GPWS Too Low Flaps Alert. The PM immediately called for the go-around and it was initiated. A quick scan of the flap handle and I realized that I had neglected to call for Flaps 30 from a Flaps 15 setting; and the Before Landing Checklist was not run. We were quickly vectored around for another uneventful ILS. The minor distraction of adding a gust factor and changing the Autobrakes setting sent me out of a normal approach configuration sequence and we ended up both missing the final flap setting and subsequent checklist.With the normal ZZZ procedure of not contacting (and often not switching to) Tower until reaching the fix at about a 5-mile final; it's possible for us to miss out on cues to changing weather on the ground. We were number 3 or 4 in a sequence of landings on 04R. Had we switched to Tower five or more miles sooner; we may have caught the gusting winds earlier and had more time to type in the adjusted winds and send away for the performance numbers prior to 1;000 feet AGL. This was another series of events combined with mild fatigue that led to poor task prioritization on our parts.
column123: The short version is that we got 'Too Low; Flaps' at 500 feet because we were at Flaps 15. We went around. Planned winds were 070/7. Tower winds were 070/12G20. I re-ran the performance landing numbers at 1;500 feet; but did not override the METAR and got back the numbers for 070/7. The PF (Pilot Flying) chose to add 5 knots to VTarget and we agreed to go from Medium to Max braking and continue.There is no guidance I am aware of as to when we would be required to re-run our landing numbers. I should not have tried to re-run the landing numbers at that point. That would have eliminated the distraction that led to not selecting our planned final flaps or running the Before Landing Checklist.

column121: On approach at night visual to Runway 26 in the Jacksonville. I was the pilot monitoring with the first officer as the flying pilot. We were given a visual approach and he [turned] off the auto pilot at approximately 1;000 feet. At the 500 feet call he was stable; I don't recall the exact altitude after 500 feet as I was heads up looking outside and I noticed three red and one white on the PAPI. I did observe he was getting low on the glideslope. We had the ILS backed up and when I looked up; I saw four red and instructed him ' four reds; level off' we also received the oral warning glideslope at the same time. He did respond and leveled off approximately two to three seconds later and I saw the three red one white again. Landing was uneventful.It is also worth noting that we were into a 13 hour duty day due to weather delays and rescheduling.Also; worth noting was both Approach Control and Tower stated that they were receiving PIREPs of 40 knot headwinds until a 4 mile final. In actuality the winds were gusting up to 40 all the way to about a 1 mile final.

column121: While on an OE Refresher flight; I was the PM and my FO student was on his first PF leg in about 9 months due to being non-qualified during the COVID crisis.We were on a right down wind with a clearance to descend 2;000 feet. We were told to stop our descent and we leveled off at 2;600 feet. ATC alerted us to a helicopter crossing our path at 2;000 feet. We reported him in sight and ATC turned us for a right base. When we were clear of the traffic we were cleared to 2;000 feet and then for a visual approach with the airport in sight.  The FO called for gear down just outside the marker and managed the speed at the marker. We were at 170 knots at the time.  We had planned for a flaps 3 landing. With our encounter/interruption with the close traffic we didn't take into account that the marker was 3.4 miles from the runway and at 1;200 AGL. With the glide slope captured and a planned flaps 3 landing the airplane was having a hard time slowing down. I pulled the speed brakes to assist the aircraft and the FO was slowing the airplane down.  I pulled them just inside the marker and stowed them a few seconds later when the airspeed was near Vref. What I didn't realize was I had left them extended below 1;000 feet.  I think part of the issue is the approach seems a little nonstandard (standard: 5 NM; 1;500 feet AGL) and sneaks up on you with the marker at 3.4 NM from the runway at 1;200 feet.

column121: Landing Runway 26 Using Visual approach guidance from GTN 650(Garmin Navigation/Display). No glideslope guidance was provided by the GTN. I crossed what appeared to be the threshold with very faded marking in significant turbulence. I taxied to the ramp without incident. Only after reviewing the track log from foreflight on google earth did I realize I may have actually landed on the displaced threshold. I believe better markings on the displaced threshold would help prevent this in the future. In addition; a more positive identification of runway markings on my part and a go around would have prevented this event. Contributing was a runway change from our planned arrival to Runway 35R due to changing winds.

column121: While on approach to ZZZ in day VMC conditions; crew was instructed to fly direct 'ZZZZZ' - the FAF for ILS XX - for a visual approach to [Runway] XX. The crew had been left high by both Center and Approach; but was able to reach the 5;000 ft. and slow prior to intercept. The PIC/PF (Pilot Flying) briefed the SIC/PM (Pilot Monitoring) on speed/energy management and airspeed 'windows' prior to the approach. After reporting runway in sight and receiving the approach clearance; the PF initiated descent to 4;500 ft. (the FAF altitude) and began to lessen the intercept angle; and PM prompted gear down and flaps 22. Aircraft still slightly overshot the localizer but was able to capture both LOC and GS which happened simultaneously. 4;500 ft. is within 1;300 ft. AGL of field elevation; and due to the short distance both vertically to the runway and laterally (just outside 4 miles); the PM/PIC anticipated an unstable approach and called a go around. The PF pressed the TOGA (Takeoff Go-Around); disconnected the autopilot; and called 'check thrust flaps nine' and the PM set flaps 9 and checked the thrust to discover the PF had not yet increased thrust to detents. Concurrently with the 'check thrust flaps 9 actions'; and 'Sink Rate' EGPWS warning occurred; and momentary hesitation by PF prompted PM/PIC to take control and initiate the immediate action in response to the 'sink rate' AURAL as required for the EGPWS escape; with a 'Pull Up' Aural warning occurring concurrently with the initiation of the EGPWS escape procedure. (At the time of initiation the EGPWS escape; PIC noted an 1;800 fpm descent rate). Upon reaching a safe altitude; the aircraft configuration was cleaned up in line as normal; and flight continued back for ILS without further incident. One error was made during EGPWS escape; which was PIC only increasing thrust to detents and not to max. Upon completion of the flight; crew debriefed on a number of key items. These included energy management and the approach profile in the AOM; go around procedure and ensuring power is set concurrently with pitch and callout when conducting a missed; and the EGPWS escape maneuver.Crews should act very early to mitigate unstable approach situations; including in a situation like this requesting a lower altitude earlier and a vector out to the IAF in lieu of the FAF; this can alleviate the chances of an unstable approach long before the issue manifests. Missed approaches should be initiated immediately upon identification of an unstable approach. In the event of a missed approach; crews should ensure the action is taken decisively including the increase of power; and both pilots should remain involved in the process. Should a GPWS warning be received; decisive; immediate action should be taken - as per the AOM - superseding normal missed approach actions due to the time critical nature of high vertical speed close to the ground. Captains especially should keep a close eye on FOs conducting operations at outstations - in this situation; having approach offer a better vector to join or taking an actual ILS clearance may have prevented the situation from manifesting at all.

column121: At 10;000 ft. cleared direct ZZZZZ and cleared visual RWY XXL ZZZ. We both agreed 8;000 ft. was a safe altitude due to terrain then 7;000 ft. after clearing the hills. Flying pilot; FO (First Officer); set 8;000 ft. then 7;000 ft. in the altitude window. It seemed we were high since we delayed the descent so we were trying to get down. I did not notice the altitude was set to 6;000 ft. Approaching ZZZZZ I realized we were low and told the FO to stop the descent. We climbed slightly and joined the Glideslope to continue the approach. We did not get any terrain warnings but it was concerning that we descended lower than anticipated at night into a high altitude airport.The FO was low time in the airplane and had just came back from a 5 year absence. I did not realize he was behind a bit on the automation. While I was trying to figure out what he was doing I lost situational awareness even though we had discussed safe altitudes. It was also the third leg we had flown that day so we were both tired.I was overly depending on the automation to help reduce the work load for both of us. The automation will only help if it is programed correctly so a person has to be very careful to cross check all available information. From now on I think it is best to request an instrument approach when it's dark going into airports you're not 100 percent familiar with. From now on I will at the very least request to join the visual further out on the instrument approach course in order to get more stabilized.

column121: We were being vectored from the northwest to the ILS approach and descending to 2;000 ft. when Tower asked if we had the airport in sight. This was my first time in ZZZ and although I thought I saw the airport I was not in a hurry to call it in sight; just to be sure. I verbalized that with the First Officer but he had been there before and was comfortable calling the airport in sight; so I did. Our initial vector would have taken us just outside the Final Approach Fix. However; when Tower issued clearance for the Visual Approach; the First Officer turned to a heading that would take us inside the Final Approach Fix. At the same time; he continued to descend while configuring with flaps (I think at either 10 or 15) and landing gear; and the speed brake extended. As the descent continued; Tower issued an 'Altitude Alert' and instructed us to adjust immediately. I cannot remember the exact verbiage; but there was a mention of our altitude being 1;200 ft. and perhaps something about 'minimum vectoring altitude.' The First Officer immediately adjusted his attitude to level off at around 1;200 ft. and continued to fly towards the runway center line. We were approximately abeam the Final Approach Fix at this point. He then asked for more flaps; ultimately Flaps 30; for the landing. I realized at about that point that he still had the speed brake extended and told him. He immediately stowed the speed brake and intercepted the visual glide path to the runway. I think he asked me at about that point if I was okay to continue. He was on the glide path and stabilized; so I said 'yes.'On landing rollout I noticed what sounded like an open microphone on the Tower frequency. Since there didn't seem to be any other aircraft on the frequency; I suspected it was coming from our aircraft. The Tower made a broadcast to all stations on the frequency that they were broadcasting on an active ATC radio frequency; and that they should cease immediately. When I made the '80 kts.' call out on landing roll; I heard myself over the radio. That confirmed that the First Officer was holding down the microphone button in his grip on the yoke. As soon as he loosened his grip; the transmission stopped.
column123: I was the pilot flying. Based on the weather reported we briefed the RNAV Arrival to be followed by the ILS Approach. We made special note that we were not to utilize the Autothrottle for the approach due to MEL(Minimum Equipment List) restriction. The arrival went as planned until we were given a vector off the arrival and then direct to the Final Approach Fix and a descent to 2;000 ft. As we were a bit high and fast; the speed brake was being utilized to expedite descent. While proceeding to the Final Approach Fix; the weather was acceptable to proceed visually so we reported field in sight and were cleared for the Visual Approach. Due to the high intercept angle to the Final Approach Fix I turned to establish a more acceptable intercept angle to capture the localizer. My perception looking outside was that we were quite high so asked the Pilot Monitoring to set Touchdown Zone Elevation. I failed to engage Vertical Nav for better situational awareness in relation to my vertical path and relied solely on the visual sight picture until on final. While on a dogleg to final we received an ATC Altitude warning alert telling us that we were below the sector altitude. We were approximately 1;100 ft. just abeam of the Final Approach Fix on a dogleg to final. I then stopped the descent until approaching final approach course and engaging Approach mode for glideslope capture. Continued the Visual Approach to a landing.

column121: I was enroute to ILM. As I began my descent into the Wilmington terminal area the ATIS and Approach Controller indicated ILS Runway 06Z was the approach in use. Weather was reported as 400 overcast and one mile visibility. Winds 030 degrees at 4 knots. A cold front and convective activity was approaching the airport and I requested a short vector to final. I was cleared for the approach and began my descent. I ended up too high on the approach and executed a missed approach at minimums. As I was being vectored back for another approach I was advised that the aircraft following me also executed a missed approach due to being too high. I looked at the wind vector on my PFD and realized that I had a 50 knot tailwind at 1;500 feet on my last approach. I advised the Controller about the strong winds at altitude and asked if Runway 6 was still the best runway.I also asked the Controller if he had any real time winds on the ground and was reminded that the Tower was closed; he would try to get me an update from the automatic reporting system. I was told that the automatic reporting system still indicated winds out of the NE (northeast) at 11 knots. I continued for a second approach but at 700 feet AGL my wind vector was showing a 50 knot tailwind and my groundspeed confirmed that. I deemed it unsafe to land and executed another missed approach.The Controller suggested ZZZ as an alternate. I began vectors to an approach to RNAV GPS Runway XX. I quickly realized that I would have the same 50 knot tailwind situation at ZZZ and began questioning the Controller about any real time wind information on the ground. He said the only info he had still indicated winds on the ground were out of the NE about 7 knot. He said the convective activity situation at Wilmington was improving and asked if I would like to go back for another approach. I was beginning vectors to another ILS Approach 06Z. I was still seeing the same 50 knot winds at altitude and began doubting the wind reports on the ground. I asked for an approach to Runway 24. I was cleared for the RNAV approach 24. As I was descending on final approach; I had a 50 knot head wind at 1;000 feet.When I reached minimums and began to go visual; I encountered a windshear. I increased power to MCT (Max Continuous Thrust) trying to climb out; but to no avail. As I was continuing to recover from the windshear; my landing gear contacted a treetop about 1/4 mile from the runway. At this point I deemed the safest course of action was to continue to land. I made a normal landing to a full stop; turned off the runway and taxied to the FBO. After exiting the airplane minor damage was noted. At this point the winds on the ground had shifted to the SW (southwest)) and were gusting to 35 kts indicating to me that I had landed during frontal passage.The biggest contributing factor to safety I noted; was the lack of any real time winds on the ground. If I had real time wind data from the Controller as I was conducting my approach; I would have been advised of the wind shift occurring; and might have been able to take preventive action before I encountered the windshear. My recommendation is to not have a system that solely relies on the automatic reporting system which is not real time. I would have each Approach Control have the ability to see and give real time winds just as an operating Tower would have.
column125: Pilot reported contacting tree tops during landing at ILM airport due to windshear.  Pilot stated there was no real time wind information.

column121: While on final approach the PF was having difficulty slowing the aircraft and continuing the descent. The headwind we had been fighting quickly turned to a crosswind. The PF elected to re-bug and use flaps 40 well above 1;000 feet. As the PF I selected the new flap setting in the FMC and verbally called out the Vref and target speeds. As we were slowing past our previous target speed the PF called for flaps 40. We had no amber band visible for flaps 40 placard speed; so I verified the placard; 162 knots; and selected the flaps seeing 158 knots and slowing on my tape. As I verified the speed brake was armed he again called for 40. The flaps were still at 30. At this point we were now below 1;000 feet but above 500 feet. We discussed the non-movement and load alleviation. As the PM; I suggested; pitch up and slow; the flaps moved to 40 as we suspected they would. The checklist was completed and we were able to be stable by 500 feet AGL; so we continued the landing. We did not observe any limitations being exceeded on the flaps or in procedure as the PM was able to manage the approach prior to the 500 foot gate.In discussing this event during our debrief we believe that we did meet our approach gate criteria. We were in our first 'heavy' approach in many months as the flight was full. The weight may have contributed. The wind was also a contributing factor as it shifted down the final enough that by the time we departed the traffic flow had reversed.Neither of us were averse to going around but it did not seem necessary in the moment as it seemed we met our criteria to continue. Having said that I'm certain eyes further from the instrument panel may have captured data we missed but I believe we trapped and corrected the situation.
column123: While on final approach I; PF; called for final landing flap (flaps 40) and flaps did not move to commanded position (they were at 30); despite being slower than max speed for flaps 40. Since we were within a few knots of max flaps 40 speed; I slowed further and flaps extended to commanded position. I believe the flap extension occurred right around 500 feet.  The final approach speed was within stabilized approach criteria and the outcome of the approach and landing were not in doubt. Landing was uneventful.We have been flying mostly empty airplanes for almost a year and this flight was almost completely full. I believe the higher landing weight coupled with a significant loss of headwind on final contributed to the airplane taking a while to slow down to an acceptable speed for final flaps. When I called for final flaps; we were 3-4 knots below the max flaps speed and I believe the aircraft's safety system was restricting flap movement until I was a little slower. As we return to Pre-COVID passenger loads; I will have to adjust lead time for decelerating the jet. Today was a good reminder.

column121: I was pilot monitoring on this flight. ATC was giving us vectors for a visual approach to RWY XX at ZZZ. We were at 2;000 feet on a right base between waypoints ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1 (1;500 feet MSL) when we told Approach we had the airport in sight. We were cleared for the visual and told to contact Tower. I said 'glide slope alive' and the First Officer asked for gear down flaps 15. I looked down to select the Tower frequency (which I didn't have preselected) and told them we were on the visual for RWY XX. We were cleared to land. When I saw we were capturing the localizer I noticed that the glide slope was now full scale deflection (low). The First Officer had selected a lower altitude (1;100 feet) on the MCP without telling me and so we were below the final approach fix altitude of 1;500 feet at ZZZZZ1. I called for a go around and we were vectored back around for a successful landing. The cause was a long flight across the country with last minute vectors to final for a visual approach led to the First Officer thinking we were high nearing the FAF and incorrectly setting an altitude lower than glideslope intercept altitude without verbally advising the Captain.

column121: Private pilot student working toward a commercial certificate; practicing a simulated engine out within 1 mile of the runway from 2;200 AGL. The student was guiding the aircraft toward the runway. We had passed the threshold and were still high. I (the instructor) was directing coordinated S turns in order to not overshoot the available runway. We were using Runway X with winds approximately 330 at 7kts. On the last turn back toward the runway we were just west of the runway and I then noticed that airspeed was getting too low; we attempted to still land the plane; at this point only about 20ft above ground. We continued the descent and were touching down on the runway but not aligned with the runway. The student initiated a bank to realign the aircraft with the runway and the left wing apparently scraped the runway without any indication on the flight controls. I took the controls and leveled the wings; controlling the aircraft through a bounce. We departed the runway at an angle into the grass median between the runway and taxiway A. We continued the ground roll in the grass; crossing taxiway G before coming to a stop.  Upon shutting down and inspecting the aircraft we found the scrape on the left wingtip and corner of the left aileron.

column121: Visual to 27 at SAN using RNAV Z. Ceiling was BKN015. Crew briefed visual procedures at altitude but was informed that ATIS had changed late in the descent; crew adjusted minimums to account for the updated ceiling. Earlier I had queried the Captain if he preferred flaps 40 for SAN and he said he wanted flaps 30. Vref was 141 knots; wind component added 6 knots for a VTARGET of 147 knots. Captain configured early and we were stable at 1;000 feet AGL with a slightly high VVI (Vertical Velocity Indicator) due to SAN Runway 27 3.5 degree approach and the high weight and subsequent high approach speed.  We were visual at that point; I think we broke out at 1;000-1;200 feet. Every time I crosschecked the speed and VVI; the Captain was stable; with the knowledge that the 3.5 degree approach and speed (as mentioned in the Safety Alert) would result in VVI rates over 1;000 feet. I was crosschecking radar altitudes and glideslope guidance more than I usually do; but this is my personal habit for SAN. As I did the normal 'in and out' visual crosscheck; nothing seemed amiss. Directly over or slightly after the parking deck on short final; the Captain slightly adjusted his visual aim point as is common technique at SAN. My eyes were outside at this point. We both heard a 'sink rate...'. I don't know what the second phrase was; as the Captain acknowledged the first sink rate forcefully and almost instantly. I quickly looked inside and I think I saw 'pull up' on the bottom of the PFD for what seemed like an instant. I can't be certain; however; and it can't be understated how fast it went away if indeed it was there. I looked back up and we were two red and two white on the PAPI; slightly fast; and Captain was in what looked like a good position to land. We made a safe landing and applied moderate braking and exited the runway. In the postflight; the Captain and I disagreed about what the second term was at the aural system annunciated. He thinks he heard 'sink rate; sink rate.' I actually don't know what the second term the aural system annunciated was; as I didn't hear it clearly due to the Captain's acknowledgment of it. I do think I saw a very fleeting 'pull up' on the PFD; but I can't be perfectly certain; and Captain is adamant that we didn't get the GPWS warning; only the sink rate caution. I want to be clear that I understand the limitation with regard to the warning annunciator. In hindsight; I can see now that a go-around could have been a prudent decision even though the aircraft appeared to be a safe and normal position for short final at SAN. I want to highlight this issue for a few reasons. It bears repeating that phase of flight; temporal distortion; and flight deck confusion can all conspire to create a situation where two trained and alert crew members can see different things. The other thing I want to highlight is how heavy -800's; going into SAN; can potentially be affected by a flaps 30 approach. I don't know why we don't have recommended flap settings for certain situations. We've all been flying light aircraft for almost a year now and perhaps it would help to remind crews about heavy aircraft with higher approach airspeeds and subsequently higher VVIs in higher altitude environments or steeper approaches like SAN.
column123: On approach to SAN when transitioning outside; we briefly received a sink rate call. At the gate; the First Officer was unsure if it was just a sink rate call or sink rate pull up. I adjusted sink rate and landed normally.

column121: This was me and the First Officer's first flight together.  He hadn't had a landing for approximately a month and hadn't been flying a lot recently.  It was a beautiful day in ZZZ and we were cleared for the visual to XXL at about 7;000 MSL.  We were established on the localizer as a backup and had the runway in sight holding around 210 knots.  Approach control handed us off to tower and I checked in.  We were cleared to land and told that there would be fighters departing out of ZZZ1; but they would stay below us.  The First Officer started too slow and I extended flaps to 2.  I started searching for the fighters and saw 2 of them takeoff.  I keep my eyes on them to make sure we were clear.  First Officer called for the gear and I extended it.  I noticed we were still at flaps 2 and we were too fast for flaps 3.  We were inside the FAF by this point and it was going to be close for a stabilized approach.  The first officer had previously selected the approach mode and we were on course about 300 feet high on the glide path and fast so not correcting.  I noticed the power on the engine instruments was not at idle which was strange because we had the approach selected in managed speed and the speed tape showed the target speed well below our current speed.  I pointed the power setting out to the first officer and it was also confusing to him why the power was still up.  We got to flaps 3 around 1;000 AGL and were still high on the glide path.  We leveled off a little to get the flaps to full and then started to correct to glidepath.  When we got to 500 feet we were still a good 200 feet high and 15-20 knots above our target speed.  The First Officer asked me if I wanted him to go around.  There was no traffic and the runway is 2 miles long so I said to continue.  We ended up touching down at target speed well within the touchdown zone and cleared the runway.  After getting to the gate and shutting down the engines we debriefed the approach.  The First Officer said that all along he thought he was setting himself up nicely for the approach and thought it would work.  I agreed and then I took myself out of the SA loop when I started searching for the fighters and didn't pay close enough attention to our energy status.  When I finally did get back into it I wasn't forceful enough to help.  When I pointed out the engines not being at idle I assumed he would disconnect the auto thrust and put the throttles at idle.  When he didn't I should have told him too and that alone would have probably made us stable as there was still some time.  At any rate I didn't and we were clearly unstable and we both knew it.  The only correct option was to execute a go-around.  Fuel was not an issue as we ended up landing with 6;500 pounds.  I told him that I should have told him to go-around but he should have just executed a go-around without asking.  That is the procedure.  You don't need permission from the other pilot and permission not to go-around is still wrong anyway.  We agreed that the biggest factor that got us going down this path in the first place was his recency of experience and that in order to fix that he should in the immediate future at least; slow down and configure earlier then he thinks he needs to.  Going back to our departure briefing I should have taken the first leg anyway since I knew he hadn't flown in a while.  This way he could have eased back into it.

column121: I operated Aircraft X from ZZZ1 to ZZZ. The weather was low; approximately 600 OVC at ZZZ and rain. I believe we had an alternate of ZZZ2. We planned to shoot the ILS to Runway 7 at ZZZ. I was the pilot flying. We operated the flight to ZZZ normally and got set up for the ILS to Runway 7. I was established on the approach and fully configured at approximately 1;000 feet when I noticed that both the localizer and glide slope were flagged. I was looking at the PFD (Primary Flight Display) and slightly startled by the lack of information. I then glanced to the First Officer's side and noticed his PFD looked normal. I never felt any erratic pitch attitudes but according to FOQA (Flight Operations Quality Assurance); we reached 12 degrees pitch down and up to 2700 FPM descent below 1;000 feet. I remember hearing the aural 'glide slope glide slope' The FO (First Officer) then questioned; 'should we go-around?' I responded yes and initiated a go around. We climbed up to 3;000 feet and requested radar vectors back around for the same approach. I then transferred the controls to the First Officer because his navigation equipment was operating normally. We continued on the approach. At approximately 600 feet I called runway in sight; and he responded landing. We drifted to the right of the approach course; due to the First Officer not reacting to the left crosswind. We were also a bit high on the glide slope. I told him to correct; and asked him if he was able to correct the flight path; he responded correcting. We ended up landing hard and fast. I taxied off normally. I forgot to write up the discrepancy that the airplane lost localizer and glide slope. I believe the cause of this event was due to the gradual failure of the glide slope and my reaction to that. It took us approximately 38 seconds from when the failure occurred until the missed approach was executed.  I don't think it would hurt to see this event repeated in a simulator so that I can see it again and understand how I could have reacted better.
column123: I was Pilot Monitoring for an approach into ZZZ1. We were conducting the ILS to runway 7 at ZZZ1; and had just passed the final approach fix when the Captain's glideslope malfunctioned and presented a red 'GS' flag. My instruments appeared to continue to provide accurate glideslope indications; and the autopilot remained engaged; coupled to the Captain's side; and continued to fly the aircraft. I don't recall noticing which vertical mode took over in the absence of the GS. I suggested to the Captain that a go-around be made; she waited several seconds to see if the glideslope indications would return; then disconnected the autopilot to execute the go-around. The Captain applied TOGA (Takeoff/Go-around) power. Upon applying go-around power; the pitch attitude further decreased; and the vertical rate of descent correspondingly increased; causing a GPWS sink-rate warning. Visual ground contact was made briefly before the descent was arrested. We continued the climb straight out and contacted Approach for missed approach instructions. This event began with the malfunction of the Captain's side glideslope. This led to a breakdown in situational awareness and crew communication as the aircraft continued to descend in the absence of the glideslope until a go-around maneuver was executed. I believe additional training may be required; both on go-around procedures as well as on equipment malfunctions during approach.

column121: Turning for a 7 to 8 NM final from left base to Runway XX with aircraft configured flaps 20; PF established aircraft on ILS Runway XX GS and LOC for visual approach. PM made a VFR traffic call for an 8 NM final landing Runway XX. Another jet on the ground at ZZZ made multiple calls for a radio check and the PM responded he could hear the jet loud and clear. PF called autopilot off; and request the visual approach for Runway XX in the FMS. The PM entered the Runway XX visual approach into the FMS.  PM made a 5 NM final call on CTAF. The jet on the ground informed the PM they were taxiing on Runway XX and would clear the runway prior to aircraft landing and then engaged the PM in a conversation about their inability to contact ATC Clearance. PF called flaps 30 and flaps 45 and slowed the aircraft on speed and Glide Slope. PM read the before landing check and at the LANDING GEAR DOWN call both the PF and PM noted the lack of DOWN 3 GREEN on the display panel and then visually checked the gear handle and noted it was in the up position. This occurred at about 1;100 feet HAT and was follow by the gear horn going off. PF immediately began a go-around and made call outs PM preformed G/A TO actions and traffic calls. The aircraft descended to 900 to 1000 HAT before entering the upwind for Runway XX and performed an uneventful left traffic pattern landing on Runway XX.This was my first day back from a month of leave and my Captain had also recently returned from leave. Rust may have been a factor. Additionally; we were flying at a high altitude airport with terrain at night with no Tower. There was definitely a communication break down and I was tasked saturated and should have let the Captain know I was in the red; and I could have missed the gear call. The final distraction was the jet on the ground taxiing on Runway XX where they should not have been; and fixation on them clearing the runway and engaging in a conversation about contacting ATC to get their IFR clearance was also a factor taking my awareness outside the aircraft.In the future I need to be more aware of my saturation level and communicate this correctly to my captains and avoid engaging in extraneous ground communications with other aircraft. Also; I need to visually and verbally verify my actions and confirm call outs by myself and other pilots regardless if I am PM or PF and verbally verify ambiguous calls or situations.

column121: Turning final ZZZZ fully configured at 150 knots I armed the Localizer first than the Approach mode. First Officer inquired and received the landing clearance. I noticed that the glide slope didn't capture and that we were going above the path. I tried to descend in Vertical Speed; but it didn't come active; so it wouldn't work. Approach mode light still on. I disconnected the Autopilot; lowered the nose and started a descend using the VGSI (Visual Glideslope) path indicator and the outside PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator). During the descent we tried to reset the Approach/Glideslope unsuccessfully. The approach was pretty bumpy with some good downdrafts. The aircraft went below the glide slope and I started to correct back when the pull up warning came on then stopped right away. Then the glide slope warning followed. I was just under the path recovering and had 3 red lights and 1 white on the PAPI. Speed around 10 knots above Vref and decreasing toward Vref. I was very focused on the approach and continued to an uneventful normal landing.We were configured early; ready for approach; but the Glideslope not capturing and the uncertainty concerning the landing clearance created a distraction that put me behind the aircraft and I developed some tunnel vision trying to remedy the approach. I became fixated and continued when I should have discontinued the approach and gone around. After landing the First Officer said to me that she had mentioned going around; but it hadn't registered with me during the approach. I tell First Officers that there is no mission; but today I didn't follow my own advice. It's easy to become over focused without realizing it. When in doubt; go-around.
column123: The event began while beginning the ILS Approach. The company notes on the approach dictate not to arm the ILS until reaching a certain position because of the possibility of false localizer signals. The Captain was the Pilot Flying and I was the Pilot Monitoring.  I remember thinking that the Pilot Flying armed the approach later than I would have since we were well beyond the designated point. My best guess on what happened is that because of the delay the localizer armed but the glideslope never captured because we were already too high. Whatever the reason for the automation not doing what we had expected; the result was that we realized that the Glideslope never captured and were trying to get the aircraft to come down.Not only were we distracted with being above the intended glide path; but we were handed over to tower late and I was busy with getting clearance to land while the Pilot Flying was flying the plane trying to get back to a stabilized approach. As the aircraft was getting back to a normal glide path (3 red; 1 white on PAPI) around the 500 foot mark I gave my stabilized approach callout and realizing we were slightly above speed and correcting; but the vertical speed exceeded what is acceptable. At this point I said 'I think we should go around'. I can't remember if it was just before or after I said this that we got a 'PULL UP' alert. The Captain elected to continue the landing. Later after discussion; I learned he may have had tunnel vision to the point where my go around call didn't register with him.My unfamiliarity with ZZZZ did not help our situational awareness as well as being distracted with getting switched over to tower (late) and attempting to get landing clearance around the same time we realized the automation did not perform as expected. As a FO/PM not being assertive enough when a go-around was needed.Abiding by company SOP to the letter. No matter what; at 500 feet if stabilized approach criteria aren't met; it's an automatic and firm 'GO AROUND'.

column121: Flying a steeper than usual glide path to a runway with displaced threshold. During transition to visual I inadvertently descended below the glide slope to achieve a normal sight picture. Received a Ground Proximity Warning and Glide Slope. Too much focus outside the aircraft. We were over the touchdown zone and a normal landing was completed. Attention inside and outside the airplane during transition to landing flare.
column123: We were in the flare over the runway and received the 'Glideslope' EGPWS advisory immediately prior to touchdown. Aural Glideslope advisory while we were in the landing hover/flare. The First Officer hand flew the Visual Approach and maintained the Glideslope until we were approaching the threshold and he shifted his attention to the external environment. The Glideslope and PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator) for this runway may have been the cause when he transitioned to flying by outside reference and attempting to achieve a sight picture closer to our typical 3.00 degree glide path. We were over the touchdown zone and completed a very normal and uneventful landing.

column121: Arriving in gusty crosswind conditions. VMC. I was hand flying the ILS. At runway threshold; I inadvertently descended below the glide slope during transition to outside and received a glide slope warning. As we were in the flare over the runway landing was the safest option. [Identified by] GPWS warning; glide slope. Began transition to outside too early preoccupation with winds. Failed to continue cross checking indications. I heard the annunciation in the landing flare and completed a normal landing. The flight director indications guide the airplane to the touchdown zone use this information in combination with outside.

column121: Very light 757; 16.7 on the fuel; almost empty payload; operating from ZZZ to ZZZ1; daytime VMC. High winds in effect. Vref was 111; Vapp is 126. I was PF (Pilot Flying); and decided to fly faster on final since we were in a light 757; and my limited experience with this situation led to this. Missed approach procedures were reviewed multiple times as other jets had to go around several times. Runway was dry; over XX;000 feet long; plenty of distance. I decided to fly a new Vapp of 135; as the jet was having problems controlling the airspeed since the winds were about 060/22G30. Our divert was ZZZ1; and we knew that several aircraft attempting to land had to execute missed approaches. All this into account; I decided to fly around 135 KIAS; as this felt like a more stable approach. The airspeed wouldn't stabilize safely below this number; as it was all over the place and I had to fight with the autothrottles to maintain within 15 kts of the initial Vapp of 126--it just wouldn't stabilize at a speed below 135. My reasoning is that since I was light; in a 757; long runway; daytime VMC; and super gusty winds; I had plenty of room to stop safely without encountering hot brakes or running out of concrete. We landed uneventfully; although long; as I touched down past the touchdown zone--this was the only cost of flying faster; and I felt it was worth it; all things considered. We landed and were able to taxi clear on taxiway; no problem.  If I was in a 767 or a heavier 757; I wouldn't have even thought of this; and we would have flown the Vapp given; for better or worse.  Were we technically unstable? Yes; I believe we were. Were we safer; in my eyes; flying a more stable approach with the winds we had? Yes; I also believe this to be the case. We never had a windshear warning; and we never felt unsafe; but looking back; I was in the wrong; even though I felt more stable; which led to a safer approach. However; the AOM procedures don't allow for this; so I was technically in the wrong; even though the airplane flew better and safer; in my opinion. Next time; I will fly the Vapp given; and if we have to divert; then so be it.

column121: Normal departure and flight enroute to ZZZ with thunderstorm approaching ZZZ airport on arrival from the west. Radar vectors for ILS XXL for approach; multiple speed adjustments from ATC on approach and no reports of wind gusts on initial arrival from ATC with winds 180/6 from ATIS. Storm had moved over field when on approach. On short final there was an RVR (Runway Visual Range) update from recall of greater than 5;000 RVR and moderate to heavy rain but no winds passed from recall. VMC around 1;100 feet and could see right and center runway but then went into torrential rain downpour around 400-500 feet on short final where Captain made an EFIS lights call and then I shortly after picked up runway lights and landing environment just prior to minimums and was a little high but was adjusting in close. Around 30-40 feet we had an instantaneous airspeed loss of around 10kts and at that time I had decided to go-around but with the airspeed loss and instantaneous loss of lift we touched down as go-around decision was made. We had a semi-hard landing and bounced and go-around selected all simultaneously. We were slow to put the speed brake back in after touching down but did eventually secure it and maintained attitude as we executed go around. Go-around procedures were completed airborne. Vectored back around by ATC for uneventful approach; landing and taxi in. The Captain and I both did a walk around of the aircraft post flight to ensure integrity of aircraft and check tail skid as a precaution but didn't notice the skid was ground down as we later were informed from the Director of Operations and Maintenance. We debriefed the event. Windshear/airspeed loss at low altitude while simultaneously executing go-around and didn't maintain proper go-around pitch once touching down as indicated from tail skid damage. Quicker reaction from crew with go-around with instantaneous airspeed loss and loss of lift and maintain proper attitude of aircraft on touchdown when executing go-around. Follow rules to live by; do not make large control inputs when in flare/go-around close to ground.

column121: When checking in with MCO final Approach Control we were given speed reduction 190 to mkr (Marker Radio Beacon); Drones reported near ORL Executive; all traffic now arriving Runway 17L and R. Cleared visual Runway 17L maintain GS on approach slow to 170. Caution traffic on right to Runway 17R; contact Tower on freq. We received our landing clearance for Runway 17L. Second birds on path siting. We were on ref and GS. About 1;100 ft. I realized we weren't fully configured. Called for gear/F3 then F Full/Landing checklist. Landing checklist completed by 800 ft. We were still at ref and on GS fully stable; we landed normally and taxied off at Taxiway F. We received no alerts during approach.I believe we were distracted more than usual as well as feeling a little tired that afternoon. I will most assuredly use this experience as a learning example of avoiding potential complacency by carefully applying our procedures to prevent any re occurrence going forward.
