column121: This flight was the first leg of a four day for the Captain and I. The Captain said he would be the pilot flying; that I would be pilot monitoring and he briefed everything up; including threats. We made each other aware of our lack of recent flying experience and lack of proficiency. I informed him that I only have a little over 700 hours on type; that most of that time was trans-cons prior to the pandemic and that I haven't flown much in the past twelve months. I also informed him to the best of my knowledge this would be my first time flying into ZZZZ [Airport]. We agreed that we would take things slow and that we were doing everything correctly. Our taxi out; take off; departure and cruise were routine and uneventful. Approximately twenty minutes prior to the Top of Descent the Captain briefed our descent and approach into ZZZZ and we ran the descent checklist. Center gave us a descent; followed by descend via the X Arrival. We were handed off to approach and told to expect the VOR [Runway] X; after ZZZZZ [Intersection] to fly present heading for a vector to the VOR approach. Prior to ZZZZZ the Captain called for flaps 1; After ZZZZZ he called flaps 5; slowed to 190 knots and approach gave us the vector and cleared us for the VOR X approach. We were at or near the final approach fix when the Captain called gear down; flaps fifteen followed by flaps 30 landing checklist. I set final approach altitude; we were VOR/LOC and VNAV descending per the approach and slowing to our vref of 156 knots. At 1000 feet we were fully configured; and I believe we were at 165 knots. I called 1000 feet; the Captain was task saturated so I said 1;000 feet; set missed approach altitude and said cleared to land. I noticed VNAV had kicked off; I verbalized it and re-selected VNAV; I thought it went to VNAV PTH; but in our debrief I learned it went to VNAV SPD. Between 1;000 and 500 feet the fastest speed I saw was 170 knots. At 500 feet I called 500 feet and saw 171 knots and almost called a go-around. I called 15 knots fast; the Captain said correcting and I saw the speed slowly begin to decrease. I was focused on airspeed as we were at the limit for a stabilized approach and I was looking at the runway as I was concerned; we were going to be high. We continued our approach and I was beginning to become uncomfortable; at approximately 200 feet I noticed we were at 170 knots (it may have been a couple knots faster) but 170 is what I recall.  At approximately 200 feet I called that we were high and fast; the Captain said correcting and I saw the speed slowly begin to decrease and he pitched down to correct. At approximately 100 feet I wanted to call for a go-around as we were fast; 170 knots or faster; however I was unable to verbalize it. We touched down hard and flat; we may have had a small bounce; but I don't believe that we did. The Captain deployed the reverser's and we made a normal exit onto taxiway and proceeded to parking. Upon parking; we debriefed this event; we both conducted thorough post flight inspections of the exterior. To the best of our knowledge the aircraft was nott damaged. The Captain contacted Maintenance and they wrote up the hard landing. The Captain also called the Duty Manager.Factors that led to this event were both of our lack of recent flying experience; unfamiliar airport; night operation; Runway X PAPI out of service; the change from VNAV PTH to VNAV SPD and I did not call for a go-around.I have learned from this event and will call for a go-around if I become uncomfortable or we become unstable in the future.
column123: We were flying into ZZZZ [Airport] on the VOR [Runway] X approach at night. I had not flown for a year. Currency was a factor.  While on approach I configured on time and landing checklist was called for and completed.  At 500 feet we were 15 knots fast (171 knots) and the FO said we are fast. I said correcting. At 200 feet I was 17 knots (173 knots) fast the FO said we are fast; I tried to correct.  Also; at 200 feet the FO called out high. I over corrected and landed abruptly from 50 feet to touch down. I do not believe this was a bounced landing but may have been. Our pitch was flat; and we landed main gear first. Our descent rate from 50 feet to 0 feet was extreme.  The failure of myself or as a crew to not call a Go Around was the primary mitigating factor.  Also executing an off center VOR approach with a PAPI OTS at night was a factor. I felt I was not high but in reality; it was due to lack of visible surroundings. Furthermore; the VOR approach only gave guidance to 1;054 feet MSL. To further complicate issues the VNAV disconnected. So; in essence we were left minimal visual guidance. Winds were 20 knots reported and was another factor. My FO stated he was about to call for go-around at 500 feet and 200 feet; but neither of us did. After landing we taxied in normally. We both did several post flight inspections. I also notified Maintenance and the Duty Pilot of the events. This was the first leg of a 4 day with an FO I've never flown with. All briefings were completed. The FO and I discussed before departure how infrequently we have flown due to the pandemic. He had never been to ZZZ [Airport]. I had not been in 2 years. All approach briefings were concluded in a timely manner. The STAR in use was the X arrival. After ZZZZZ [Intersection] we were instructed to fly present heading for radar vectors to join the VOR X LOC. The VOR approach X is a very tricky approach. It is offset with limited visual guidance. Flaps 1 was initiated prior to ZZZZZ. Flaps 5 was initiated after ZZZZZ. Best to our knowledge we were configured by the FAF. The FMA was showing VOR/LOC and VNAV path commencement to approach; at some point we lost VNAV path and went to VNAV speed. I accomplished a LAVS check but did not add 2 and 4 mile rings. I may have gotten very close to exceeding flaps 30 speed. I also believe my flare was minimal at best. Having a PAPI would have been beneficial.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported an unstable approach and a hard landing.  Both pilots reported lack of flying as a contributing factor.

column121: Neither one of us had ever been into ZZZ. We both took the opportunity on the flight to review the company pages. Acknowledging that it is a special qualification airport and after review of company documents; we realized that controls needed to be transferred to the Captain due to my low time in the aircraft. Control of the aircraft was transferred at approximately 13;000 feet MSL. Traffic was landing XXL. We briefed and set up for the RNAV for XXL. Center handed us off to ZZZ Approach. We were asked to advise when the field was in sight. Conditions were VFR and remained so. We had the field and were cleared for the visual. We recognized immediately that we were high. We had a tailwind which made it exceptionally difficult to get configured. The Captain continued to descend and try to reduce speed. He called for gear; however; I was unable to extend the gear because we were not at the appropriate speed. We were switched to Tower. The Captain continued the descent all the while attempting to reduce speed. Best guess we were at approximately a 7 mile final. We received an EGPWS 'TERRAIN TERRAIN'. The Captain shallowed the descent. It was then followed by a 'TERRAIN TERRAIN PULL UP' and I called for the go-around. We followed the escape procedures; were vectored around the pattern; and landed without incident. Neither pilot had been into the airport. We had reviewed documents; however once cleared for the visual we did not comply with the prescribed speeds on the arrival. In retrospect; we should have continued on the RNAV arrival since we were unfamiliar with the airport. Additionally; better awareness of wind may have assisted in getting configured.

column121: Runway 16L; the controls were transferred to me by my First Officer (FO).Oakland Center handed us off to Reno Approach; on initial contact we were advised to report the field insight for the visual approach to 16L. After calling the field in sight; I realized that we were a bit high and closer to the field than I had anticipated. Calling for gear down; my first officer called 'speed check I realized that with speed brakes extended; and all efforts to slow the airplane; with a quartering tailwind; my efforts were futile. Still descending and approaching position WORTH on the RNAV approach we were handed over to tower who cleared us for landing on Runway 16L. Being unstabilized at this time and receiving an EGPWS 'TERRAIN TERRAIN' followed by 'PULL UP" my FO and I simultaneously announced "GO AROUND.'We executed the EGPWS escape maneuver and were vectored for another visual approach; followed by an uneventful landing to Runway 16L. Never having flown into the RNO airport; having an FO just off OE (operator experience) and new to the aircraft type; gusty winds; tailwind on initial descent and approach. On landing winds were 260/variable. On arrival at the gate we were told by station personnel other aircraft had also gone around a one diverted. Suggest more emphasis on speed control throughout the descent and approach."

column121: During the approach briefing for the ILS approach to Runway 15 at LCH; the First Officer (Pilot Flying); informed the Captain that he had problems with the Runway 15 localizer on two previous flights for this runway. The FO (First Officer) said that on both previous flights; the Runway 15 localizer did not stay centered. The FO explained the Localizer Course Deviation Indicator (CDI) had swung left and right of course. After discussing this; the Captain and FO agreed that the FO would remain on GPS guidance while the Captain would monitor on the localizer radio guidance. After being cleared for the approach; The FO intercepted the inbound course on GPS guidance. The Captain's localizer guidance initially appeared stable. Approximately three miles prior to the final approach fix; FIDGA; the FO switched his course guidance to the localizer. The approach guidance was stable. We passed FIDGA; intercepted the glide slope; and began our final descent. At approximately 1;300 feet MSL; the localizer began deviating left and right of course. The autopilot was engaged. The FO disengaged the autopilot and we immediately descended below the cloud ceiling; which was approximately 1;100 feet MSL or 1;200 feet MSL. The Captain and FO both sighted the runway at 1;100 feet MSL. At some point below 1;000 feet MSL; the localizer appeared to be stable. The remainder of the approach was uneventful. During the approach; the winds were 40 knots from approximately 200 degrees; prior to the FAF. After clearing the runway; we reported the problem to the Control Tower. The FO informed the Tower Controller he had problems with the Runway 15 localizer on two previous occasions. The Tower Controller said he had not received any other notifications about problems with the localizer from any other aircrews. At no point was the approach unstable. [Request] inspection of the localizer antenna. A query to the LCH Control Tower to determine if other aircrews have experienced the same problem. A notification in the daily read warning aircrews they might encounter the same problem.

column121: When on arrival and approach the flight was put 5nm in trail of a 757. This caused the aircraft to experience wake turbulence intermittently for the last approximately 100nm of the flight. While focusing on mitigation strategies for the wake; a loss of situational awareness occurred causing the pilot flying to become high and fast on approach. Aircraft was configured by 1;000 feet AGL; however was still stabilizing airspeed. Aircraft landed without incident. Increased spacing between heavy jets and regional jets would reduce the pilot workload on arrival and approach and thus minimize the chance of fixation on that issue.

column121: While flying I failed to fully configure by 1;000 feet AGL. Approach was a Visual Approach. Conditions were clear; 10-mile visibility; and light winds.When landing on Runway XX; TRACON had a restriction to cross a point at 2;600 feet. Configuration at that point was Gear and Flaps 15. Began the descent but had trouble initiating the descent due to automation configuration. During this phase of the approach for some reason I thought I had until 500 feet AGL for final configuration during a Visual Approach. Final configuration was accomplished by 700 feet. Later I referred to the Operating Manual and realized my mistake.Causal factors [included] a lack of recent flying. Aircraft was a B737 MAX and this was the first time I flew one in over X years. This aircraft does not slow down and go down like the B737 NG thus adding to configuration delay. Failure to remember that ALL approaches require final configuration by 1;000 feet. Pairing had a XC:10 Sign-In requiring me to get up at XA:30.  Although this occurred at approximately XI:00  I had already been awake for 9 hours; some of it on the back side of the clock. Perhaps have the same criteria for both IMC and VMC as far as being 'in the slot' this would eliminate any confusion at all. Bottom line; I confused the issue and will not allow that to happen again.
column123: Approach Control told us to maintain 2;600 feet until north of a point.  The Final Approach Fix is south of this point and the altitude is 2;600 feet. The requirement to maintain 2;600 feet caused us to be high on approach. Passing 1;000 feet we were not stable and should have gone around. I felt Approach Control had set us up for failure. The Captain had a lack of recent flying experience and hadn't flown the MAX in two years. He thought being configured by 500 feet on a Visual Approach was acceptable. After landing we checked the manual and realized being stable by 1;000 feet is a requirement for all approaches. It's difficult to be stable by 1;000 feet with the restriction approach control gave us. This restriction is impractical and makes our job difficult.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported final configuration was not accomplished until 700 feet which is not in accordance with the manual.

column121: During the approach briefing for RNAV GPS 35 at AVL; I had pointed out to the Captain that due to obstacles and terrain characteristics in the vicinity of AVL; ATC would keep us high until very close to the station. As anticipated; Atlanta Center kept us at a high altitude (I do not remember exactly; but well above 15;000) until about 12-15 miles from the station. Upon being handed to Asheville Approach; and considering the terrain; night time; and the need for sufficient airspace to descend to a reasonable altitude in order to execute an effective and stable approach; I requested radar vectors for the RNAV GPS 35 (glideslope for the ILS was out of service). Approach instructed us to descend to 6;000 and to a heading that would put the aircraft on a downwind to Runway 35.  Subsequently; the Approach Controller gave us a heading that put the aircraft on a left base leg to Runway 35; and instructed us to descend from 6;000 to 5;100. During this descent; the GPWS issued an aural TERRAIN caution. We immediately initiated a climb; advised ATC of the situation; and stopped the climb at 6;000; at which point the GPWS silenced. In addition; we advised we had the field in sight; and were cleared for a visual approach to Runway 35.  The minimum sector altitude corresponding to the left base leg to Runway 35 stated 6;200 ft. We should have queried ATC of the validity of the instruction to descend from 6;000 to 5;100.  From a CRM standpoint; The Jeppessen AVL Airport Reference Page alludes to the fact that a high rate of descent in the vicinity of the airport may cause a GPWS activation due to mountainous terrain in the vicinity (We did not exceed 1500 fpm); hence an emphasis on this matter during the approach briefing; in conjunction to minimizing the rate of descent to; perhaps; no more than 700 fpm; more than likely would have avoided the situation.

column121: We were on final approach for Runway 35 at PHL conducting a Flaps 22 visual approach and landing. Conditions were VMC and we were configured for landing by 1;000 ft. and stable by 800 ft. in accordance with policies requiring the aircraft to be configured by 1;000 ft. and stable by 500 ft. in visual conditions. We were notified by the Tower that an aircraft was landing on the intersecting 27R runway and that we were cleared to land on Runway 35. During my landing flare as we crossed the [Runway] 27R intersection we encountered wake turbulence from the previous arrival. The left wing suddenly dipped and I felt the aircraft enter only what I can describe as an enhanced ground effect where the aircraft was not sinking at the rate that it was prior to crossing the intersection. Due to this condition I felt as though the aircraft would not be able to touchdown in the Runway 35 TDZ and I called for and executed a go around. We completed the go around maneuver successfully and were vectored back around for a visual approach to RWY 27R where we landed uneventfully and taxied to the gate.The FO (First Officer) and I felt the wing dip and the aircraft began floating and my sink rate had ceased.I believe the cause of the wing dip and enhanced ground effect was due to wake turbulence from the previous RWY 27R arrival. This all happened as soon as we were over the [Runways] 27R/35 intersection in the landing flare. The aircraft was sinking normally during my flare prior to crossing the intersection.Feeling as though a touchdown in the touchdown zone would no longer be possible I elected to go around.Prior to this event I have landed on [Runway] 35 many times after an arrival on the intersecting runway and felt the wake of the landing aircraft. In this instance the effects of what we encountered far exceeded what I have ever felt before. Perhaps more information could be distributed to the flight crews regarding any other similar events if there is a pattern of this occurring.
column122: Reporter stated more ATC awareness is needed in the area of possible wake from intersecting runways.
column125: Air Carrier Captain reported executing a go-around after encountering wake turbulence from previous arrival on an intersecting runway.

column121: I was the PM (Pilot Monitoring) for the flight and first approach. It started with a C HYD QTY (Center Hydraulic Quantity) EICAS in the terminal area of ZZZ. The QRH was completed. A RNAV GPS approach was conducted with the autopilot and autothrottles off. Weather was day/VMC. Immediately after gear and flaps were extended there was a complete failure of the C hydraulic system with the associated EICAS messages. I announced to the PF (Pilot Flying) that we would have to abandon the approach and complete the QRH. I coordinated a go around with Tower. I explained our problem and our need to perform the QRH before another attempt to land. While talking to Tower; I failed to monitor our airspeed after the First Officer (FO) arrested the rate of descent. I had not given him any guidance as to maintaining a particular speed or altitude. The stick shaker got my attention. I called 'Speed' just as the PF added power and recovered quickly.Tower handed us over to Approach. I explained the need to perform the QRH; the time needed to complete the QRH; and that we would only have 2 of three hydraulic systems. A few minutes into working on the QRH procedure ATC advised us that they were [giving priority to] us. ATC asked for souls on board and fuel state. The QRH was completed. I elected to become the PF for the landing. The landing and rollout were successful. Emergency vehicles escorted us to parking.Of course I wish I could do it over. I'm not happy with my initial performance with the problem. The system failure happened at the most inconvenient time. Nonetheless the decision to not land and fix the problem was easy enough. However; my initial execution was poor.  After announcing the problem to the PF; I should have simply called Go Around. That would've put us back into the 'Aviate' phase and eliminated the stick shaker activation. Then I would've told Tower of the Go Around. Everything else would have sequenced properly for the remainder of the flight.
column123: The Captain was PM and I; the First Officer was PF for our flight. Takeoff; climb and cruise were uneventful and no issues or indications were observed until descent into ZZZ. After obtaining the latest weather at ZZZ we decided Runway XX would be the most suitable based on the winds (270/3) and we would plan on and request the RNAV XX into ZZZ. We loaded the approach; requested landing performance via ACARS and briefed it as a normal flight.Descending into the area required the use of the speed brakes in order to keep the aircraft on the established VNAV path as well as slow down to 240 kts when passing 10;000 ft. Once reaching 9000 ft on a downwind position we first saw the C HYD QTY EICAS message appear. The PM checked the status page and noticed a magenta 'RF' and an indication of 0.45 for the center hydraulic quantity. The PM ran the C HYD QTY QRH and the only information listed was 'the hydraulic quantity is low'. We completed the checklist; called for recall review; cancelled it and discussed that we would write it up once on the ground in ZZZ and advise maintenance of the issue. We continued our descent for the RNAV XX approach; reducing speed and configuring for the landing. We were issued a heading and to maintain 3000 till established and we were cleared for the approach. I had setup for an approach as briefed; set 1200 in the altitude window and the aircraft began a shallow descent in VNAV PATH down to the minimums. After calling for 'gear down; flaps 20';  I set 160 kts in the speed window and the C HYD SYS PRESS EICAS message appeared. I called for 'flaps 25; landing checklist' when the PM alerted me of the EICAS message. After this a series of additional EICAS messages appeared and discrete lights illuminated. I noticed LEADING EDGE; TRAILING EDGE; and TE FLAP ASYM as the associated discrete lights had illuminated as well and got my attention. As the list of messages and indications appeared; the PM stated 'We can't land like this; we're going to need to find a place to go figure out what's happened'.As the PF; I had set 146 as the target speed for the approach and had just disengaged the autopilot and authrottle as briefed on the arrival briefing for automation intent. After the PM made that statement I hesitated to continue descending as I knew we could not land in this condition. The PM advised tower that we had an issue and would need to stay in the traffic pattern in order to troubleshoot the problem and would not be able to land at this time. At this moment I noticed the low speed awareness tape had not continued trending downward and minimum operating speed (red and black tape) was now very close to our current speed. Shortly after the stick shaker came on for roughly one second as the airspeed trended into the minimum operating airspeed range. I immediately added thrust and added forward pressure on the control column to increase airspeed and stop the stick shaker. The PM called out 'Airspeed' as I was taking corrective action. ZZZ tower informed us we could climb to 3000 ft. to abandon our approach and to run the necessary QRH. The PM set 3000 in the altitude window; we both verified it and I pressed the GA (Go Around) switch on the thrust levers. We conducted the GA profile; when bringing the flaps to 20 I could feel the asymmetric condition as the aircraft pitched up and the thrust increased. Continuing with the GA the PM raised the landing gear up; we maintained runway heading and set a speed of roughly 185 kts in the climb out. During the climb out the master caution light was intermittent as the gear doors and landing gear indications were intermittent as well. I remained PF with the autopilot disengaged and the auto throttle engaged. The PM attempted to engage Right Autopilot Command but we noticed this did not initially work so I continued flying manually with no autopilot. ZZZ approach advised us to proceed direct to the ZZZ1 VOR and stay within 10 DME while we ran the QRH and advised them of our intentions. At this time ATC asked for souls on board and fuel remaining and we advised them we had 2 souls on board and roughly 2 hours of fuel. The PM ran through the C HYD SYS PRESS QRH; noticing the center hydraulic quantity was down to 0.15 at one point. With no success completing the QRH; we completed the descent and approach checklist from the deferred items followed by the alternate flap extension and alternate gear extension checklists. The leading and trailing edge flaps successfully extended to 20 and the landing gear was down and 3 green lights were illuminated. At this time the PF and PM switched roles as the captain stated he felt more comfortable conducting the approach to landing. The captain stated the briefing remained the same and talked about the different Vref as a result of the new flap setting. After a positive transfer of controls; I advised ZZZ approach we would like to return to ZZZ and conduct the RNAV XX approach. We were vectored on a left downwind for the approach at which time the PF reduced speed to the target speed of 156 based on a flaps 20 Vref of 151; while I completed the landing checklist from the deferred items. We advised ATC we would be able to land the aircraft normally; they informed us that ARFF (Aircraft Rescue & Fire Fighting) would be standing by the runway just in case. We landed Runway XX and exited at taxiway 1 and told tower we could taxi to the international cargo ramp without any additional assistance. We left the flaps down at 20 after landing in order to prevent any further issued with the flight control movement. After blocking in on the ramp; maintenance came up and informed us that a substantial amount of hydraulic fluid had leaked onto the right main gear. We completed the necessary post flight paperwork; placed it in the trip envelope and then conducted a post flight inspection speaking to ARFF and maintenance about the exterior of the aircraft. At this time the captain and I grabbed our flight bags and headed to the Company transport to leave the airport.Looking back on the event I think there were a few things that could've been done differently. The initial indication of the C HYD QTY should have been a sign that something may have gone wrong; and after running the associated QRH we could've perhaps asked to slow and configure early to identify if there was an issue while at a higher altitude rather than on final approach. My role of PF on final approach. Despite the indications; discrete lights and overall confusion that was occurring at the time I should've had focused solely on flying the airplane and not getting distracted by the other things going on. This may have prevented the stick shaker event from occurring. There was no clear command to go around once we encountered the C HYD SYS PRESS EICAS message. Rather than wait for instructions or what the Captain was trying to diagnose; I should've called go around instead of remaining at 2500 ft with the airspeed trending downward in that configuration. As the other crew member I could have voiced my opinions in a more assertive way; stating what I believe may have happened as well as consulting the Captain if he felt we should identify as an emergency at the time.

column121: We were flying back to ZZZ on a clear dark night; arriving on day four. It was my leg (Captain); PF (Pilot Flying). About 20 miles out we were given a vector to ZZZZZ which is about 6 miles south of ZZZZZ1; the final approach fix for runway XXL. The controller asked us to keep our speed up to 210 or better until final as she had other inbound aircraft that she was sequencing in. We switched to green needles and sequenced the approach according to our normal procedures. When we were about 10 miles from ZZZZZ; approximately abeam ZZZZZ1; we called the field in sight. The controller gave us a heading of 080; cleared us for the approach and switched us to tower. She noted that the other aircraft would be following us and to keep 170 or better to final. At this point we were about 5 miles from ZZZZZ1 at about 90 degrees intercept. The heading the controller gave us would take us inside the final approach fix before we intercepted the final bearing. Seeing this; the FO (First Officer) noted that we had not begun our decent to final approach fix altitude yet. I called for the descent; flaps 20 as we slowed to final approach speed. As PF; I noted that the flight guidance would not give us a good intercept because the intercept was too great. At this moment; the autopilot began the turn to final too soon. I turned off the auto pilot and began hand flying. As we began the turn to final I called for flaps 30; and then flaps 45 as we neared the final bearing. As we were completing the turn; the FO (Pilot Monitoring) noted that we were still high and that we should increase our rate of decent; we were about 2000 MSL; I steepened our decent and he selected 1000 fpm as we rolled out. I called for and we completed the landing checklist. Tower came up and checked us in. We had not called at the FAP (Final Approach Path) as we were busy flying. At this point; we were about 4 miles from the end of the runway at about 1800 MSL. The tower gave us landing clearance and noted that we were low as the PF I called for us to stop our decent; and I leveled the aircraft at about 1500 MSL. I noted that I had not completed the turn correctly and we were not quite on final bearing. I corrected and called for zero VSI (Vertical Speed Indicator) and adjusted to get on final. The FO complied and we continued inbound. At about 3 miles from the runway on final bearing our GPWS alarm went off; PULL UP. As PF; I called for a climb and began a climb at about 5 degrees nose up. As we began our climb; the GPWS alarm went off again; saying PULL UP; I increased the climb; and we climbed about 200ft; as we were very close to glideslope. I was about to call for a missed approach; when I noted that the climb had put us on glideslope; and the PAPI's confirmed us on glideslope. Seeing this; I reestablished our decent and the FO called stabilized at about 3 miles. We landed normally; completed our checklists and shut down the aircraft in accordance with our normal procedures. We have procedures and training guidance that should have prevented this. In this case; there were several errors I made on this approach. As PF; I should not have accepted the vector inside the final approach fix with such a large intercept heading. As Captain; I knew that the flight guidance would be unable to make the turn correctly and we would need more room to make the turn to final. I should have asked to continue our course and give the controller other options. This likely would have prevented the whole series of errors. I should have started my decent to FAF altitude sooner. We were at 3;000 feet; 5 miles from ZZZZZ1 as we turned; and I delayed our decent to 2;400 ft. for several seconds as I turned off the flight guidance and began the turn. If we had been at 2;400 MSL we would not have required a large decent in the turn to final. When the tower checked us in at 4 miles and noted that we were low; I should have called for and established a climb back to glideslope instead of just leveling out. We were very close to glideslope and we should have re-intercepted it first. When the GPWS alarm went off; I should have immediately called for a Missed Approach. We were low (hence the alarm) and there is no space or time for altitude errors on final. Any delay calling for a Missed Approach puts safety at risk. Finally; there was probably some 'get-home-itis' in our minds as we were flying. Arriving late at night; at home field on day four; we were tired and wanted the trip to be over. Going missed would have delayed our landing. Also; we were also trying to satisfy the controller who was sequencing us in front of another aircraft. We needed to focus on our flight; not her decisions or problems.
column125: Captain reported an uneventful landing after an unstable approach resulted in a CFIT event during final approach.

column121: First Officer; Pilot Monitoring. Captain; Pilot Flying. 3;000 feet MSL inbound to AWLEE 210 KIAS or greater assigned. Cleared for a visual approach approximately 5 miles from AWLEE 170 or greater to 5 mile final assigned. Continue on heading; allow spacing for visual approach at 210 KIAS. Abeam CRDNL given 080 heading and still cleared for visual and switched to Tower. 080 heading gave a perpendicular intercept course for final. Captain armed approach mode. Aircraft began an early turn to final simultaneously the Captain input a command that caused the plane to enter into pitch mode. I called out pitch mode and he turned autopilot off. I advised the Captain that he needed to begin a descent since we were inside the final approach fix and were 600 feet high. I entered vertical speed mode and put in a thousand ft per min rate of descent and dialed in a 45 degree intercept into the heading. Captain began slowing down and called for gear down; flaps 30; and flaps 45 at appropriate speeds I called for level off when I realized we were at 1;500 feet MSL (acceleration altitude). Simultaneously we received a call from Tower advising us that we were low for our area. I informed Tower that we were leveling and continuing our visual approach. At this point I went outside the cockpit and became focused on looking for towers that would pose a danger to our flight. I didn't recognize any threats. I thought that we could maintain this altitude and intercept the glideslope without issue. We then received a call of terrain. I said we needed to climb. No climb was initiated. We then received the audio alert of woop woop terrain. Pull up. At this point I stated we needed to climb. I began pulling back pressure and a climb was initiated. The audible signals ceased and we continued to intercept the glide slope. Once established on glide slope and on course I called out 1;000 feet stabilized and we completed the approach to a landing.  As soon as we were at 1;500 MSL I should have called for a go around. I knew the Captain was disoriented and was confident that I could act as a back stop to get him onto final. I didn't call for a go around after we stopped getting the audible warnings because we were at that point established within the safe and standard parameters of the approach. However; a go-around may have been the pertinent thing to do to increase everyone's situational awareness.

column121: Prior to making the flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1; I met with my Flight Instructor to review my flight plan and briefing which I created. All parameters were within the confines of my limitations for my student pilot endorsements for a student solo; cross-country operation. This was my first cross-county solo flight in the aircraft. I was to make two full-stop landings at ZZZ1 as part of the flight; then return to ZZZ. Takeoff from ZZZ and cruise to ZZZ1 were uneventful. I flew at 5;500 feet and reported transitioning the airspace above a non-controlled airport along the way. Immediately after reporting this transition; and well prior to entering the Delta Airspace of ZZZ1; I switched my COM2 radio to ZZZ1 and got the latest automated weather report. I do not recall what the letter designation for the report was at the time; but the weather was clear with good visibility of 10+ miles; winds relatively calm; and a clear sky. I then radioed the tower at ZZZ1 on their frequency and identified myself as a student pilot solo flight along with my relative position west of the airport and requested a full-stop landing while approximately 15nm away.The Tower answered saying they were having difficulty hearing my transmission and to restate my position. I spoke more loudly and clearly; identifying my position as west at about 14.6 miles. They advised me to re-contact them when less than a mile from the runway.  I began a descent appropriate to arrive at pattern altitude prior to radio contact. When I got within a mile I radioed the tower and the tower advised me to turn for my base and that I was cleared to land. I executed turns to base and final without issue and extended full flaps incrementally. Upon entering final approach with full-flaps I was just high of the glideslope as indicated by the PAPI system. I entered my flair and 'ballooned' slightly. When I came back down from ballooning; I came down hard and in the process; experienced prop strike; front gear collapse; and damage to my right wing before coming to a stop on the grass to the left of the runway with the help of the grass to arrest my momentum. The plane came to rest in a nose-down fashion at an approximate 45 degree angle to the edge of the runway and in the direction of the landing. After announcing over the radio that they have a crash on the runway; the tower radioed me to ask my condition and I replied that I was fine. I then noticed fuel leaking from my right wing and elected to shut down electrical items as a precaution. When the airport firetruck arrived; the operator sprayed foam on the area leaking as a precaution for the fuel leak. After several minutes; the responding airport staff strapped the nose wheel to the fuselage and were able to pull the plane to a storage hangar with a tractor by keeping the plane on the main landing gear and tugging the plane via the tail tie-down.

column121: Coming in on the OHSEA2 arrival we requested the visual to 20R into SNA. Passing over BHEAR waypoint we were asked if we had an arriving aircraft in site that was inbound from the east; I believe on the Disney4 arrival. We visually acquired the aircraft and was told to descend to 4;000 feet MSL just to the east of BHEAR. We were then told to turn right heading 030 and asked if we had the aircraft in site and we said 'yes; in site. We were told to continue the right turn to 120 and then told we were cleared for the visual approach Runway 20R. When we were coming around at 4;000 feet MSL I realized we had been vectored inside of LEMON. I had to make an aggressive descent in order to capture the Glide Path to be stabilized by 500 feet AGL in order to have a normal descent rate with approach power and approach speed captured. During this aggressive rate of descent to capture glide path (estimated over 2;000 feet FPM); Tower made an advisory call 'low altitude warning; check altitude;' we were actually above glide slope and coming down onto Glide path when Tower made this call but the steep descent rate triggered their warning system and they made a radio call for a 'low altitude warning;' we were never below glide slope/glide path.The most important take away is that this whole scenario started because SoCal approach vectored us high and turned us tight inside of LEMON. This high and tight turn is very hard to capture a stabilized approach. I would recommend that SoCal gives additional downwind distance prior to turning base leg if the requirement is to stay above 2;500 feet MSL when turning near LEMON or HUKEM."

column121: Once on final for [Runway] XXR; we got a TCAS RA event from what appeared to me an (other carrier) aircraft that overshot the final for XXL. We did not have the transponder in TA mode because we were never given a Traffic Advisory from ATC; and did not have the aircraft in sight as instructed by our guidance. The RA instructed us to Adjust Vertical Speed. It commanded approximately 1500 FPM descent. We were at flaps 10 and 1;500 feet AGL. As we descended per the RA we continued to configure the aircraft. We were below 1;000 feet by the time we got to flaps 30. The aural glideslope warning sounded. We knew we needed to go-around but couldn't because of the aircraft above us. When I looked at the ND (Navigation Display); I saw the red square showing the intruding aircraft right above us at +5. It was at 800 feet when we got the clear of conflict aural message and elected to go around.The (other carrier) aircraft; in my opinion; went through their intended final for XXL. A contributing factor was the lack of notification by ATC.
column123: While descending through approximately 2;000 feet AGL on the visual approach and on the ILS glideslope to Runway XXR in ZZZ; we received a TCAS RA Descend. The conflicting traffic was slightly above and behind us on Runway XXL. We believe the Descend RA extinguished at approximately 800 feet AGL (although a review of the FRM (Fault Reporting Manual) afterwards says it should be inhibited 'below approximately 1;100 feet radio altitude'). After following the Descend command and by the time the command extinguished; we were not in a stabilized position to land. We executed a go-around. We were not informed about the traffic by Approach and therefore did not meet the requirements to switch to TA Only for Runways XXL and XXR in ZZZ. We were vectored around for a normal landing on XXR. As we switched to ZZZ Tower; another aircraft in front of us also experienced a go-around for the same situation.Presumably the conflicting traffic met the requirements to switch to TA Only because they keyed up on the MIC and said they didn't overshoot and we should/can switch to TA Only. However; we didn't meet the requirements to switch to TA Only.  ATC never asked us to report them in sight; presumably because they were behind us! So; what happens to our RA resolution when the conflicting traffic is in TA Only and transgressing on us? Presumably they can't be offered an RA resolution (which would likely have been for them to climb). If they had climbed the Descend RA 'pressure' on us could have been alleviated sooner. Instead we were forced lower and lower. And all of this was close to the ground. Even though the RA inhibits reaching 'approximately 1;100 feet radio altitude' the conflict with the other aircraft still exists; and we were pretty sure we were below 1;000 feet before the RA extinguished (as evidence by being already slightly into the 1;000 feet white tape). When the RA finally went away we executed a go-around from about 600-800 feet; but the traffic conflict doesn't magically go away.This event occurred right as we were configuring to land; so we never had a chance to fully configure prior to 1;000 feet AGL; as maneuvering for the RA was taking up our attention. The time in the RA resolution mode was more than momentary. It seemed long and sustained enough that it felt like it would just RA us into the ground. As I now knew a go-around was imminent there was a slight moment in my mind I thought about going around right then and there; but the RA continued. I can see where a Crew may be spring loaded to go-around even though the Descend RA is still present.The FO recognized that the traffic showed '+500' on the MFD display. The FAA defines a Near Midair as 'an incident associated with the operation of an aircraft in which a possibility of collision occurs as a result of proximity of less than 500 feet to another aircraft...' We were likely very close to meeting that definition.There was a slight tail wind at altitude for aircraft joining final from left base to runway XXL allowing for increased potential for overshoot. Maybe a very slight overshoot by the other aircraft and the tight sequencing by ATC created this incident; but we can't be sure. It's possible ATC was sequencing aircraft too tight.

column121: Unexpected last minute change of runway from RNAV Runway 29R to ILS runway 11L. Winds were calm forecast to pick up out of the west at our arrival time. No digital ATIS; didn't find active runway until listening to ATIS on radio well into the arrival. On downwind vectors advised of pair of military aircraft we would follow on approach. Turned base and cleared for visual approach to Runway 11L. As we turned in towards the airport; we crossed a knoll just north of the final approach course. We were descending and crossed over the top of the knoll at about 1;400 feet AGL. The GPWS activated and announced pull up terrain; the FO (First Officer) executed an escape maneuver as he had been trained.  With autopilot off speed quickly accelerated and still in the flaps 2 configuration we exceeded the limit speed by about 5 knots for 5 to 10 seconds before speed reduced back into normal range. We advised TUS that we were missed approach and were vectored around to another visual approach and landing.Cause - Higher than normal workload; changing runways; keeping traffic in sight and terrain awareness.On calm wind days be prepared for approach from either direction on smaller airports with only one primary runway. Study terrain areas to plan for visual approach altitude you want to be at crossing them.

column121: Night VFR RNAV Y XXR approach. Approaching FAF ZZZ tower advised us of Cessna  traffic on downwind turning base for short parallel runway XXL. Traffic in sight. As we passed our FAF; fully configured and stable we saw the Cessna pointing right at us on base and both remarked 'I hope they don't overshoot'. At approximately 800 feet AGL the Cessna was still pointing at us and I could see they were clearly going to overshoot slightly above us as they turned to final. I felt it was way too close and pushed over slightly as I estimated them to be no more than 100-200 feet above us as they crossed right over us and slightly behind. As our approach was now unstable in my estimation I executed an uneventful go-around. We advised tower of the close pass. We returned for a second; uneventful approach and landing to XXR. I called tower controller after landing and he said he would contact the Cessna pilot and talk to him about the incident. Cessna not keeping us in sight and possibly lining up on incorrect runway. Light VFR traffic maintaining better situational awareness with a large air carrier with approximately XXX people on board and all landing lights; fully configured on final approach.

column119: Human Factors; Procedure; Aircraft; Environment - Non Weather Related
column121: Coming into IAD the errors started when we were intercepting the localizer for Runway 1R. I was the pilot monitoring and the FO (First Officer) was the pilot flying. Approach cleared us to intercept when we were basically already crossing the final approach course so we had to navigate back to the localizer on our own. At the same time we had been getting hit with some minor wake turbulence from the airplane ahead of us which Approach informed us was a Boeing 777. We were then cleared for the visual approach and advised of the caution for wake turbulence and to maintain 170 kts. until 5 miles out. At this time we configured gear down flaps 20. I had just recently had training in the sim for wake turbulence and remembered how dangerous it could be so I advised my FO to use caution and I believe I recommended maybe staying above their path since we had already hit a small portion of their wake prior when we were on downwind. He elected to turn the autopilot off and hand fly slightly above their glide path. We then continued down and around 100 ft. got a terrain warning. Our first reaction was this was because we weren't on glide path and the FO called out; 'visual; landing.' around 50 ft. we got another aural message stating 'too low terrain'; at this point I realized something was wrong and quickly scanned and noticed we were still at flaps 20. At this point I immediately called for a balked landing. The FO restated in a question; balked landing? I said yes and he immediately executed the maneuver. We advised ATC of the go around and they asked for the reasoning and I stated we were unstable. We continued the go around and came back to land on [Runway] 1R after some vectoring without further incident. To add to this and to summarize; we did not complete a landing checklist; we were not fully configured and were at gear down flaps 20 degrees; and our speed bug was still at 170 kts. We received a terrain aural warning that we misinterpreted followed by a second aural warning and executed a balked landing at a medium energy state with lower power setting but high airspeed. Our attention started deviating from standard starting with the poor intercept angle of the localizer. I further complicated the situation by over concentrating on the wake turbulence from the 777 and focusing too much on the FO's glide path and what I would call tunnel focusing on his flying. I believe my statements to him about the wake turbulence also led to distracting him from normal flight operations as well. He was talking through his thought process on staying a half dot high on the glide path and I was responding with statements like I agree; not necessarily breaking sterile cockpit but also causing more distraction on both of our parts. This was the first time that I had not completed a landing checklist. It has made me realize that while being aware of threats like wake turbulence is important; it's even more important to maintain a greater situational awareness and take a step back and not tunnel focus on one threat while other threats could arise. While I am glad we did not try to force a landing. This is a mistake I never thought would be possible or would happen to me. I have definitely learned a lot from this experience.

column121: I am a little rusty because I haven't flown much over the past 12 months.  We had a tailwind on final; but as ZZZ frequently does; they asked us to keep our speed up and maintain 180 knots until ZZZZZ (the final approach fix on XL ILS).  I had configured to flaps 10 and gear down before ZZZZZ.  After we crossed ZZZZZ I finished configuring but the plane did not want to slow down after flaps 25; possibly because we hit a gust or went through a shear zone.  I was so distracted by the amount of difficulty I was having getting the plane to slow down so I could call for flaps 30 that I didn't realize we were lowering the flaps to 30 just as we passed through 1;000 feet.The tailwind on final;  ZZZ's request for us to keep our speed up; my relative lack of proficiency and my not paying close enough attention to our altitude were the causes for my error.I will start configuring even earlier when contending with a tailwind on final.
column123: After fully configuring the aircraft for landing; I realized that we were below 1;000 feet.A tail wind; ATC assigning 210 knots on final; and a lack of recent experience all combined played a role in my late recognition of configuring below 1;000 feet.Configuring the aircraft earlier; and paying closer attention to procedures.

column121: On left base for visual Runway (RWY) XX; slowing to full configuration and turning final; flaps were extended to 45 degrees while flight spoilers where deployed at 1;500 feet. Speed trend vector was decreasing although thrust setting was at expected thrust setting for approach. Captain called for increased power and crew realized that the flight spoilers were not fully retracted while turning final. The aircraft speed was approx. 150 knots which was below the SOP maneuvering speed of ref + 17 knots with spoilers deployed. At this time the stick shaker activated for approx. 2 seconds and the angle of attack was lowered. The flight spoilers were immediately retracted and the stick shaker stopped. Continued to join the localizer and were on a stabilized approach by 1;000 feet and landed without any incident.While slowing to configure for final approach; Pilot Flying (PF) believed the flight spoilers were fully retracted and called for flaps 8 and 20 at 200 knots. Then the call for gear down and flaps 30 at 180 knots. PF was approaching the localizer course; called flaps 45 at 160 knots. To reduce automation workload the autopilot was disconnected and was preparing to make a left turn to intercept the localizer and glide slope. The stick shaker activated at this time because the flight computer detected a pending stall with the flight spoilers deployed. Immediately after the flight spoilers were fully retracted; the stick shaker stopped and the aircraft was on speed with flaps at 45 degrees. A left turn was made intercepting the localizer and the aircraft was on a stable approach by 1;000 feet.ATC gave direct to the final approach fix for the visual to RWY XX while still descending out of 6;000 and needing to slow to configure for landing. PF could have anticipated needing more distance to reduce airspeed; possibly without flight spoilers; and fully configure the aircraft by 3 miles of the final approach fix by descending sooner and leveling off earlier. This would have reduced pilot workload and automation management allowing for a more thorough scan of the aircraft and its systems before deploying flaps and slowing to final approach speed.

column121: We were given an ILS clearance into ZZZ in VMC conditions. A VFR airplane was at our altitude and seemed to be tracking to cross the final approach course. We both had the aircraft in sight and changed to a visual approach to maneuver behind the aircraft. At that point the airplane made a sharp right turn and we had to turn further right (away from the final approach course) to maintain separation. It seemed like the plane was on a long final for ZZZ1 and we were then at 90 degrees off of the final approach and unable to make a stable approach into ZZZ. We called the go-around with Tower and were given a heading and altitude toward the east before switching to Approach Control to come back and try it again.The main issue was the VFR traffic no one was communicating with. The crew did not fully brief the location and potential for conflicting traffic for ZZZ1 when approaching from the south. Had the crew not changed to a visual approach and been given ATC vectors; we may not have had the same issues. A go-around may not have been avoidable due to conflicting traffic but a more thorough brief of the airspace and threats may have increased situational awareness and avoided confusion. We should also have not changed the type of approach at the last minute.
column123: We were given a heading to intercept the localizer to Runway XXL and given a traffic call that was not on frequency. Controller then had us maintain heading and fly through the localizer to avoid traffic which was about 500 ft. above; crossing our path and that of the localizer track. We decided as a crew that if they gave us clearance for the visual; we could maintain separation from the aircraft into ZZZ. We were given just that and made the right turn back towards the runway and began to descend for landing. The general aviation aircraft ahead of us descended into our path and made an unexpected turn as we were continuing. I disconnected the autopilot and continued the turn towards the east to avoid ZZZ3 airspace and aircraft. Once clear of the aircraft; we elected to scrap the approach and were given vectors for another uneventful approach. We were originally on a 5 mile final to Runway XYR when ZZZ swapped runway configuration and we were then rerouted to the south to land north. We briefed the new approach and had the approach loaded and armed. With the change of runways; perhaps there was not enough time to fully brief the new approach and surroundings to point out a runway to the east that could cause some confusion. Some cumulative fatigue was present; as well as it being our third of four legs for the day added to the stress of the situation. I do think the spacing given into ZZZ was a partial factor with a GA aircraft that wasn't talking to anyone. There are steps to help mitigate issues like this; like ensuring complete situational awareness of self and the possibility of others; but ultimately there might always be general aviation aircraft out there operating around controlled airspace's not on frequency.

column121: I was returning to my home base of ZZZ from ZZZ1. I am flying my newish Aircraft X; of which I have about 19 hours in. It should be noted that most of my flight hours are from 25 years ago. I knew ZZZ had some crosswinds but I thought they were manageable and I felt I needed to push myself a bit. If I remember; the winds were about 13 at 260 (260 at 13). Part of my challenge was that they were not reporting winds on METAR only ATIS. Upon approach the winds started gusting to 19. This would give an 8.4 kt. crosswind gusting to 12 knots; something I should be able to handle. So; on the first attempt; I think my approach was OK; but I flared high; bounced and quickly realized a go-around was the best option. Not perfect but decent decision making.The second attempt is where things went very wrong. This time I came in with 20 degrees of flaps. My approach was probably a bit fast; but seemed OK as I got closer to the airport. First of all; I flared too high again; bounced; but then made the mistake to try and salvage the landing. On the second bounce I recognized that the plane was proposing and I'd have a prop strike if I let it drop again. So; I started another go-around; but the plane would not climb; more importantly I wasn't facing down the runway but more towards the left side. At this point the helicopters parked left of the runway are getting bigger in my windshield but I know I've got to gain air speed. The one correct decision I made was to keep the nose down until I gained some airspeed. Eventually I got the speed and was able to climbout. At some point in this I realized why I couldn't climb; I had raised all of the flaps! I then came around and successfully landed the plane on the third attempt. It was bad enough that after I parked; the fire department came over to 'congratulate' me on my recovery; but I really think it was to make sure I was OK.I learned the following on this landing attempt. I need more time with my instructor with landings; especially crosswind landings. I need more practice on Go-Arounds focusing on flap management and right rudder.

column121: On a visual approach to Runway 16L while descending to 7000 ft. we received a GPWS warning. I leveled off around 7;100 ft. and had the terrain in sight the entire time. After passing the hill I started a descent and was unstable so we went around.We were expecting the RNAV Y 16L but were taken off because of departing traffic on Runway 34R. This lead to an extended downwind and base to final. We were much farther out than we briefed or expected. When we finally turn final I used the RNAV 16 X approach plate for distance and altitudes. Over 'BADPE' fix the altitude is 6900 ft. I set 7;000 and started to descend my rate was too much and we received the GPWS warning. [Pilot flying suggests] when operating in mountainous terrain on an RNAV approach and you are taken off that approach see if there is another approach that is better suited for your new location and situation; and familiarize yourself with it before even starting a visual approach.

column121: I was [the] pilot flying; headed into ZZZ. It was VMC; severe clear; and in the afternoon. We were being vectored toward Runway XX in ZZZ. I was in green needles; with the [Runway] XX ILS frequency active. My FO made a comment [that] we weren't picking up the right identifier; but because it was visual; and we had just called the field in sight; I was not going to reference the glideslope anyway. I could clearly see the PAPIs and was going to reference that since I had a visual. We were then cleared for the visual for [the] runway; so I kicked off the autopilot; and began a stable descent. We had noticed the glideslope and the PAPIs were not lining up; so I made a comment I was just going to fly it visually and use the PAPIs as the guide; not the glideslope. After all; it was only being used as a back up. Well; as we proceeded along; even though I was 2 white/2 red on the PAPI; the glideslope annunciator started yelling at us (later; we found out the ILS was OTS; which explained why it was not working correctly). Instead of asking the FO to switch his and my frequencies back to standby; I elected to fly higher on the PAPIs to shut the annunciator up. I ended up being 4 white about at about 300 ft. Because the winds were calm; the runway was clear and dry; and I knew a safe landing could be accomplished; I elected to continue the approach. I still landed softly and safely in the touchdown area; and stopped in the usual amount of runway required. It was an uneventful landing.When we taxied clear of the runway; I asked Tower and asked about the frequency and asked the FO to pull up the current ATIS to see if there was a NOTAM about the ILS frequency; and sure enough; it was OTS. In hindsight; I'm not sure how I missed that on the ATIS; but I should have read it more carefully and I could have avoided this situation altogether.
column123: I was the First Officer and the Pilot Monitoring for this flight. Having received ATIS for ZZZ and setting up the approach the CA (Captain) briefed the Visual Approach Runway XX to be backed up by the ILS and I concurred and had no questions on the briefing. When going to green needles on the approach I noticed the identifier for the ILS Runway XX did not match with the identifier on the LIDO chart. I mentioned to the CA that I did not have the proper identifier and I double checked the frequency to ensure that it was correct and it was. The CA acknowledged and [said] that we had been cleared for the visual approach. During the approach the CA noticed that the GS (Glideslope) and the PAPI were not matching and informed me he was strictly using the PAPI for the visual approach. While continuing on the approach the GS annunciator was sounding a notification and the CA ended up high on the PAPI at about 100-200 ft. The CA called he was high and correcting and continued the approach into ZZZ and landed in the landing zone with no issue and was able to slow the aircraft and exit on taxiway. Once on the ground the CA asked the Tower to confirm the frequency for the ILS and the Tower responded it was released to Maintenance. At that point the CA asked me to bring the ATIS back up and that is when we both noticed the ILS was NOTAMed OTS. We discussed the approach and landing and decided to both submit reports.
column125: CRJ-700 flight crew reported landing from an unstable approach due to missing ATIS information regarding ILS OTS.

column121: We were on a visual approach to Runway XX at ZZZ.  During the approach we received a GPWS Caution 'Terrain' or 'Too Low Terrain' just after the '500 ft stable' call. As the PM (Pilot Monitoring); I failed to call for a go-around.  The PF (Pilot Flying); Captain completed the approach and landing uneventfully. Everything about the approach was normal and I have seen it many times in my 3 years here. PF was stable at 100 ft above field elevation as well as at 500 ft above field elevation; the only exception was that the winds that had recently shifted and gusting were causing the airspeed to deviate by plus or minus 10 knots; but not dramatically.  The fact that we were visual with the runway; on path and speed and properly configured; made the GPWS caution surprise me. After hearing it; I reverified that we were within all our approach criteria and it appeared safe so I did not call for the go around. However; being that it was not day VMC; I should have called it. In the future I will be sure to comply with the GPWS alert immediately and call for the go-around.
column125: B757 Captain reported a GPWS warning on final approach and elected to continue to land instead of calling for a go around.

column121: The following narrative is due to a complete lack of threat and error management and operation performance.   This was the first leg of a 4 day trip and I would be flying with a First Officer for the first time.   We were heavily delayed to start our day and weather was terrible.   High winds and windshear were reported throughout the whole Company system with several crews diverting.   Yet my release for this flight indicated nothing other than I should evaluate the use of Runway XX for ZZZ departure.   When the aircraft arrived I met the arriving Captain and he explained to me the auto pilot master warning EICAS message occurred at 1;000 feet on the coupled ILS approach into ZZZ.  The Captain stated maintenance refused to take the write ups as it was going to ZZZ1 and they would casually look at it there.   I immediately called maintenance and asked why they wouldn't take write ups and said from what it sounded like a write up was not warranted.   The maintenance controller asked if I wished to MEL the autopilot and I stated how can I?  At this point I should have immediately refused the plane; but at Company; refusing to fly gets us an immediate phone call from the Chief Pilot office.   We Were now operating in the red of the threat and error management philosophy.   On the way to ZZZ1 we experienced moderate turbulence as expected and when we turned base for Runway XY the field was surrounded by virga.  The FO and I briefed a go around and the windshear escape maneuver once more.   On final at about 1;200 feet the Autopilot Fail master warning EICAS  message appeared and the Autopilot disconnected.  I continued to hand fly the aircraft and remained stable to about 300 feet where I encountered increasing performance and the approach became unstable.   A normal go-around was executed and we called positive rate gear up; we received the windshear caution.   Immediately after we hit severe turbulence.  We diverted to ZZZ without further incident.  The remainder of the flight was performed without the autopilot.Company maintenance did not take a write up from a Captain. I wrote up the AP failure and the encounter with severe turbulence. All write-ups must be taken by maintenance.
column125: EMB-145 Captain reported maintenance refusing to accept a write up on the autopilot and departed to destination airport where the autopilot failed; causing an air turn back.

column121: I was Pilot Flying. I triggered a stick shaker/pusher at the Final Approach Fix. I recovered per SOP.[There was] a northbound squall line west of the field. A last minute wind shift caused us to switch runways. Although it was fast; we were able to set up and brief before intercepting course. ATC forgot to turn [us] on the ILS; so we went through the localizer course. This led to reactionary vectors and the aircraft doing S turns until we passed the final approach fix. At this point we were flaps 30 with the power reduced. I called for the go-around; but when I pressed the autopilot disconnect button; I became fixated on the lack of a missed approach course. (I didn't press the TO-GA button.) While troubleshooting; the airspeed continued to decrease leading to a simultaneous stick shaker and pusher. I lowered the nose and recovered per the training; paying special attention to the radar altimeter. I briefly oversped flaps 8 on recovery.Stall recovery training works. I was able to accomplish the recovery quickly without having to think about it. However; it shouldn't have gotten that far. It was my first go-around since OE. I was task saturated and in the moment couldn't remember where the TO-GA button was. We were able to handle weather; ATC error; runway changes and an instrument approach at the same time. However; because our go around was triggered at a relatively untrained aircraft state/position; I missed a critical aspect of the maneuver. I don't believe this is a common problem; but incorporating go-arounds during recurrent in various positions may be beneficial.

column121: The crew flew the RNAV X RWY to 34R at RNO in day clear weather with winds reported at 260/26G31. During the flare; the crew executed a go around due to a strong gust destabilizing the approach. During climb out; the Tower Controller called the winds stronger out of the West and suggested a visual approach to Runway 25. Although having reviewed the 10-7 Special Considerations page earlier in the flight; the crew did not recall the takeoff and landing restriction on Runway 25.The crew configured for a 5 mile visual approach to Runway 25; then briefed and commenced the approached. Descending on the approach with the terrain visible and safely below the aircraft; it became apparent that the terrain east of the airport would not allow a safe approach. At approximately the same time; our descent rate in combination with the terrain resulted in a brief 'Terrain' warning; the pilot flying adjusted the aircraft flight path and then executed a go-around. The crew then flew and landed from the RNAV X to RWY 34R. I believe rushing and a false sense of familiarity with the airport led to an incomplete recognition of all the information in the 10-7 pages. I have operated into RNO regularly but not often and always to light winds down the primary runway. Arrivals and approaches are briefed extensively and flown according to standards. The day of the event; we conducted a thorough briefing for the approach; to include reviewing the operating manual about the exact procedures for executing the EO (Engine out) SIDs at RNO and which RNAV approaches we were authorized to fly.Despite my review; to include Special Considerations/Restrictions; I did not refresh the [Runway] 7/25 restriction in my mind and in the moment I did not recall the critical restriction. Additionally at the time; I did not feel like we rushed the visual approach. However; I now realize that I was focused too much on flying a stable visual approach and landing into the wind and not taking the time to truly review the approach and missed approach. Take the time to thoroughly familiarize yourself with all the applicable notes during your airport review and briefings.
column123: RNAV X 34R to RNO in day VMC; reported winds were very close to limits (260/26 G31). Due to an extremely strong gust which destabilized our approach we executed a go around. After max effort go around and stabilized climbing clean; Tower suggested we use Runway 25. Even though we had both reviewed the 10-7 pages earlier in the flight; at that particular moment we did not recall the prohibition to landing on that Runway. After agreeing that we would look at it; I as Pilot Monitoring set up the box for a visual by extending a 3 degree GS to an extended 5 mile Center Line to give us VTI.The Pilot Flying double checked the 10-9A page and the landing app. While still considering this option; we maneuvered towards the extended Center Line and set and then descended to an altitude above the depicted altitude in that area. Our rate of descent combined with rising terrain triggered the EPGWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) to a 'TERRAIN' call. The Pilot Flying immediately altered his flight path and the terrain remained visible and well below us. It became abundantly clear that a stable approach would not be possible and we abandoned this option. We opted for another attempt at the RNAV 34R; and despite strong gusty crosswinds were able to maintain a stable condition to touchdown and landing.Things I have taken away from this event: The winds Tower reports may not be the winds you experience at the Runway. By sticking to a ridged stable approach and associated Standard Operating Procedure's mantra we kept from bending metal; violating a FAR; exceeding a limit or attempting something really stupid. And although RNO is a complex city and we review the notes every time we go there; the [Runway] 25 note was momentarily forgotten. We reviewed the 10-9a pages but somehow forgot to review the 10-7 pages in that situation. A mistake I (or my First Officer) will never make again.I firmly believe that this is a onetime incident. A few moments either way and we would have missed the gust and landed uneventfully. And I have a hard time imagining that another crew; or me again; would ever forget the 10-7 notes in a complex city. But again thanks to our training department's dogma regarding stable approaches; we are self-reporting not something far worse.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported receiving an EGPWS terrain warning during descent on visual approach into RNO and subsequently performed a go-around. The crew stated they had not adequately reviewed the notes and restrictions for the runway and this may have contributed to the unstable approach.

column121: Flight was cleared for approach into Runway XX at ZZZ. After noticing the approach was too high; the flight crew executed a go-around and missed approach. The flight crew then tried to re-establish contact with ATC but was not successful. After reaching missed approach altitude the flight crew decided to turn and make left hand traffic for Runway XX; where we made a visual approach and landing while making position reports on the local CTAF frequency. After landing; it was realized that the flight crew could have potentially violated the proper missed approach procedure or additional approach clearances into ZZZ after not being able to contact ATC. After parking at gate; Captain called ATC via phone to cancel IFR.One possible cause could be the relatively rare experience of flying into an uncontrolled field at night and the procedures that are associated with that. There was also some confusion about the type of approach the flight was cleared to execute; in this case; RNAV or visual approach into Runway XX. We believed we were cleared for the visual approach. However; being unable to contact ATC added confusion and doubt to the situation after landing. Flight delays most likely added stress as well.The first suggestion for future experiences would be to be more mindful and diligent about confirming the type of approach we are cleared to execute. This would have clarified the proper procedure when executing a go-around. A second potential solution would have been to gain altitude in order to try and contact ATC. As for the approach; the flight crew could have requested vectors to leave and then rejoin the approach when the aircraft was at a more appropriate altitude before needing to execute the go-around.
column123: After executing the go around; [we] climbed to pattern altitude to make a left traffic pattern instead of executing the published missed approach. [We were] unable to re establish contact with ATC after the go around. [We] did a visual approach to a landing. Crew believed ATC cleared for visual approach; therefore not requiring the published missed approach. [We should] clarify approach clearance with ATC [next time].

column121: During the final segment of the approach phase on short final; we hear gear up at 960 feet RA. Captain told Pilot Monitoring to put the gear down and decided to continue to land. column121: Continued landing from an unstabilized approach. We thoroughly briefed and flew the ILS 10L at FLL. We received a Too Low Flap GPWS warning below 500 feet and failed to execute an immediate missed approach. The First Officer was flying an outstanding approach and was stabilized in all parameters but I had missed the flaps 30 detent despite the fact that I had 'completed'; the Before Landing Checklist and read; and pointed to the flap indicator. I still missed it. I set the flaps to 30 and we landed. Cumulative fatigue and startle effect were definitely contributing factors. I know better than that. I should have commanded an immediate go around.
column123: Landing 10L in FLL. Approach briefed thoroughly. Stable approach the whole way. Captain completed the landing checklist at appropriate time. Read and confirmed flaps 30 but missed that flaps were at 25. At near 500 ft we got the flaps too low on the GPWS. The captain and I immediately realized the flaps were only at 25. He selected flaps 30. The aircraft and approach were stable. Seeing that we did not have much fuel and there was a lot of traffic in the area; we decided the safest course of action was to land the aircraft. The landing was continued in a stable manner in the touchdown zone. Taxied to the gate and thoroughly debriefed that the proper course of action would have probably been to do a go around.
