column119: Aircraft; Company Policy; Weather; MEL; Human Factors
column121: Upon taking the aircraft; the number 3 MFD was inoperative which; per the MEL; required the Captain to complete all flying duties. While at ZZZ the Captain noticed that the GPWS Inop; Windshear Inop; Terrain Inop; and Terrain fail message were indicated in the EICAS. The Captain called maintenance who immediately deferred the issue and the Captain completed the logbook entries. Prior to push I entered the appropriate information into ACARS which did not match the PERF INIT which the Captain entered. We verified the numbers multiple times and determined that the ACARS had to be inaccurate. We also verified our numbers with the updated AOM. After calling Dispatch it appeared that their was a fleet wide issue with ACARS. It appeared to me that some information that is generally manually input may have already been calculated in the zero fuel weight of ACARS (85lbs for crew bags). Prior to the ACARS issue we notified Ground Control to receive an off time into ZZZ1 which we were given a departure time. At that point the Captain decided to complete the paper form X. We notified the ground crew of the delay who informed us they had to leave for another aircraft and would not be back for about 30-40 minutes. The Captain decided to have them push us from the gate in the event our issue was solved prior to their return. We completed the appropriate checklists and pushed but did not start any engines. Then notified ATC of the delay to void Off Time. The Captain then called the company for approval to open the cabin window to hand the form paper copy to the ground crew. Once the form was completed we called ops; handed the W&B out the window; and re completed the before start checklist all the way through. We also discussed not utilizing ACARS W&B throughout the flight (added workload). We then notified Ground that we were ready to verify off time prior to starting engines (fuel management). They gave us an immediate wheels up time so we started the engines and taxied to [Runway] XX. I used my IPAD to verify weather at ZZZ1 prior to push which was reporting calm winds ; few 050 TCU and 10 miles vis and again I checked prior to taxi which they were reporting calm winds; 046 CB; 10 miles. On departure from [Runway] XX we were given a turn to the west and eventually a heading to the north with a climb to 8;000 [feet]. When given direct to ZZZZZ for the ZZZZZ4 Arrival we had to vector around a few storms. We also had to vector around storm clouds on the arrival and were given vectors off the arrival by approach due to flow into ZZZ1. Within this period we both discussed the weather; verified the TAF; and discussed needing an alternate and potential fuel issues. After updating the D-ATIS we verified that the field visibility had dropped below 3 miles so we messaged Dispatch asking for/if we needed an alternate due to visibility. Dispatch responded that the TAF was good and we didn't need an alternate. The Captain started to become concerned because he was showing extreme areas of precipitation along the arrival and approach. Other aircraft ahead reported heavy rain. There were multiple discussion with ATC about getting in to ZZZ1 but I don't remember if they advised aircraft were going missed. We received normal vectors into the approach to Runway XYL and my first awareness of wind shear is when the aircraft in front of us called wind shear and missed. With our Windshear capabilities inoperative we were both paying very close attention to the aircraft's instruments. After intercepting the localizer and passing the Runway XYL FAF the precipitation was worsening. Somewhere between 1;700 and 1;200 feet the aircraft instruments were showing increased gains and losses and began to vibrate significantly. The Captain called wind shear and we completed the wind shear procedure up to the missed approach altitude of 3;000ft. Tower gave us runway heading and eventually turned us to the west with a hand off to approach. Prior to the handoff we began discussing fuel and alternates. The Captain suggested ZZZ2 which we messaged to Dispatch. Dispatch recommended ZZZ3. I recommend that we not go to ZZZ3 because of all the weather that was building between ZZZ1 and ZZZ3 and it also appeared weather was building at ZZZ3 per my IPAD. We asked ATC about ZZZ3 and they indicated that ZZZ3 had similar weather to ZZZ1 so we determined based on fuel and ATCs information that ZZZ3 was not an option. It seemed to me that Dispatch was adamant about diverting to ZZZ3. We also discussed other airport near ZZZ1 (off field) that could be potential alternates. After the check in with Approach they asked us our intentions which we replied stand by and began rough fuel calculations to ZZZ2. The Captain quickly determined that fuel to ZZZ2 was inadequate due to a 15-20 minute flight and approximately 2200lbs of fuel on board. (Increased fuel burn at lower altitude; storms in the area; and potential unknown issues on arrival at ZZZ2). We then asked ATC about aircraft success in to ZZZ1 which they replied that some aircraft were making it in and some were not. We then requested Runway XYR because it was the closer runway and informed ATC of our minimum fuel situation. The Captain notified the Flight Attendant; we ran the appropriate checklist (ATIS; build it; bug; brief it) and continued vectors to Runway XYR. There were two aircraft in front of us which one was able to land and another missed (aircraft in front of us) due to wind shear. After recalculating fuel requirements the Captain determined that a missed would put us in an emergency fuel situation therefore we [advised ATC]. I believe that upon intercepting the localizer we were somewhere around 1700-1800lbs. During the approach the gains and losses of altitude did not seem significant but it appeared to be difficult to maintain the localizer although I did not see anything greater than one dot. There was also added pressure to continue the approach due to the fuel situation. During the approach tower asked the aircraft that made it in where they broke out and they replied 'right at mins'. At around 800 feet I called out that I could see the ground which was not visible as we got to 500 ft. At 300 feet I called approach lights in sight 12 o'clock and continued to call out the approach lights. The aircraft began to drift right and I called the runway environment insight at 11 o'clock. Throughout this period it was apparent that the Captain was having difficulty seeing the runway. When he finally looked up and determined that he did not have adequate references; and the aircraft was not in a safe position to land; he called the missed. Immediately on the missed it was apparent the at least 90 percent of the airfield was VFR. We could see all of Runway XZ/XA; including approach paths; so we made a decision request XZ and due a VFR pattern into XZ. Tower provided we could use whatever runway we needed so we continued with XZ; entered the left crosswind; base; and landed. During the miss we did not climb to the missed approach altitude; left the gear down; and kept the flaps at nine because of the immediate landing after the missed to Runway XYR. We updated the FMS with Runway XZ and extended a line for reference because there are no approaches in our pubs for XZ. We did not rerun the performance information again because the field length was 10;500 [feet] which was longer than Runway XYR and it was an immediate landing. We also received fuel EICAS alerts during the missed and a fuel critical prior to landing XZ. All approaches were conducted with flaps 22. Emergency vehicles were on scene which we did not require. Upon taxiing into the gate the field went in to a ground stop due to lightning. In all the entire flight segment from door closed to door open was 3.4 hours. I would say that this flight provided the crew with several threats. To name a few; my PFD was inop so only the CPT could fly; windshear; GPWS; and TCAS were inop; pop-up weather wasoccurring throughout the flight route; fuel considerations; ACARS not accurate due to new AOM changes; delays prior to departure; delays in route; other aircraft encountering wind shear; and potentially an inexperienced dispatcher. There was also a significant pressure to land the second approach. I felt that we handled the situation very well as a crew and discussed and analyzed any and all potential alternatives but were cornered due to the fuel situation. I do wish I had analyzed the winds better throughout the duration and been more aware of the weather at the field during the Runway XYL wind shear event. With most of the airfield being VFR it was absolutely possible to successfully complete approaches to the XB runways and XZ/XA [runways]. The only area impacted by the weather was the XY runways approach segments. Therefore; if winds were within landing limits we could have immediately requested an alternate runway from ATC.
column123: While conducting the setup check in ZZZ; the GPWS Inop; Windshear Inop; Terr Inop; and Terr Fail messages appeared on the EICAS. Maintenance was called and the affected system was deferred per MEL. The aircraft also had an MEL for the First Officer MFD; and an NEF item in the cockpit. ACARS would not produce accurate weight and performance numbers; and Dispatch informed us that this was a known issue. We elected to perform a manual weight and balance with the Form X. The TAF for ZZZ1 was forecasting VFR and radar looked fairly benign. The First Officer obtained the D-ATIS for ZZZ1 which showed Winds Calm; visibility 10SM; Few at 5000; Scattered at 8000; with no other weather (information M). Due to this; extra fuel for weather was not considered by us or Dispatch. While in our initial descent the First Officer and myself noticed the weather in ZZZ1 was deteriorating and sent an ACARS to Dispatch asking if we should get an alternate. Dispatch replied that no alternate was needed; and we assumed that they had more up to date information than available to us in the air. Our radar was depicting areas of extreme return (red and purple) blanketing the approach and airport with other aircraft saying it was a smooth ride; but heavy precipitation. While outside of the final approach fix on the ILS Runway XYL; other aircraft were heard going missed due to windshear. ATC informed us that some aircraft were landing and some were going around. We elected to continue the approach as we were not experiencing any abnormal flight conditions. Inside of the final approach fix; I was unable to maintain positive aircraft control on the approach path due to airspeed; altitude; and course fluctuations. I made the determination to execute a windshear escape/go around. The Flight Attendant was notified of our situation. We contacted Dispatch to request a diversion and suggested ZZZ2. Dispatch replied that ZZZ3 was a better option; but ATC informed us that ZZZ3 was having similar weather to ZZZ1. I used the FMS to determine a rough fuel burn to ZZZ2 as 1400 lbs not including any deviations or approaches. We were also low on fuel; approximately 2;200 lbs after the missed approach. We discussed and agreed that we did not want to end up in ZZZ3 with such low fuel and similar weather issues. We decided to attempt a second approach at ZZZ1 after ATC stated that some aircraft were landing successfully. It was apparent that we would be below divert and possibly final divert on the second approach so we declared Minimum Fuel. We requested ILS Runway XYR due to perceived improved conditions and the closer approach for that runway. During the approach; the Flight Attendant called and said that a passenger was in the bathroom and asked if we could delay the landing. I advised her that due to our fuel situation; it would not be possible. She was able to get the passenger back to his seat before final approach. While on the approach; we overheard the aircraft in front of us go around for windshear. Our fuel level was low and a go around would put us in a fuel critical situation. We [advised ATC] and continued the approach. We experienced difficulty in maintaining the approach course and I used my PIC authority to continue the approach since a missed approach would leave us with inadequate fuel for much option. The First Officer called approach lights in sight; and we continued to 100 feet above the field. The First Officer called Runway in sight; and when I looked up; I did not see a runway and was disoriented due to the extreme precipitation and reduced visibility and immediately executed a go around. We were now showing a very low fuel level; and it was unlikely we would have enough fuel to get to another airport or reattempt an approach. I used PIC emergency authority to request a circle to land Runway XZ. We were able to maintain visual contact with Runway XZ during the maneuver. We had no time to get any type of performance numbers; and reasoned that since itwas longer than Runway XYR we could land. The landing was conducted without incident. As we engaged E/TO Reserve on the windshear escape; I entered this in the logbook and contacted maintenance. See attachments for reference if needed.Many threats existed in this incident including: pop up weather; weight restrictions; the need for paper performance calculations; an inoperative MFD; an inoperative Windshear system; and lack of an alternate. The weather and TAF looked ok during the planning portion of the flight; it was not until enroute; that everything deteriorated. I believe we acted in the best manner possible given the stressful situation and lack of backup options (fuel and/or alternate). It was a unique scenario that required a unique response. I believe we made safe decisions based on the current parameters. In hindsight; I would have been more assertive about requesting an alternate enroute. I do not believe we could have forecasted such bad weather; which is why we did not ask to remove passengers to add fuel in ZZZ. As we could not find a suitable alternate; we had little choice once we were low on fuel than to attempt to land on any runway. A third go around would have likely had us considering an off field landing.

column121: I was Pilot Flying operating the ZZZ flight. On short final; we received a sink rate aural alert. My First Officer called continue; I agreed; and we continued. It happened around 100-200 feet. We never observed more than 1000 fpm on the VSI; and the PAPI indicated 2 white and 2 red and the landing was made safely in the touchdown zone. Once the runway was assured; I elected to nose down slightly coming over the threshold to give myself some more room to get the airplane safely to the ground with the given conditions. Gusty winds; and bumpy conditions made for a more difficult approach; but at no point did it seem unsafe to either one of us.After landing in ZZZ; left hand brake temperatures were relatively high upon exiting the runway and entering taxiway A. We landed Runway XXL. Visual approach; winds 250@18G30kts. Once on taxiway; left hand outboard brake temp indicated around 380C and the inboard was around 340C; temperatures still climbing. Quick decision making and thinking; I as the Captain decided to notify Ground Control that our brake temps were too high to park at the gate; and that we would like to continue taxiing around the airport to get them to cool off enough to allow us to park safely. A few moments later; temps were above 400C and climbing; and we got the BRK OVERHEAT amber EICAS message. Called for the checklist; and complied. Basically; do not set the parking brake. The highest temp seen was 495C where it stopped climbing and finally started trending back down. I elected to taxi single engine; and only use the right hand brakes (which were under 300C the entire time) in order to get the brakes to cool off and to prevent an unsafe condition at the gate with hot brakes and the possibility of having a brake fire. Once cooled off below 300C we parked at the gate; and the message had cleared prior from the EICAS.

column121: The following narrative describes an event that took place while operating a passenger flight  - the second segment of a two-segment day. For this particular flight; the Captain (CA) was the Pilot Monitoring (PM); the other First Officer (FO) was the Pilot Flying (PF); and I was positioned in the First Observer's seat as the additional crew member; aiding in pilot monitoring duties. The flight itself was uneventful and could be described as a straightforward intra-European flight. We continuously checked the weather on our way to ETAR in order to remain cognizant of the weather conditions expected for landing. Well before the top-of-descent; we pulled the final weather report; requested aero-data performance; loaded the expected ILS approach; and once all crewmembers were present on the flight deck and ready to discuss the descent/approach/landing; the PF conducted the approach briefing. All checklists were completed on schedule and as we approached ETAR; we confirmed with approach controllers that we would expect the ILS to Runway XX. As we came within approximately 20 or so miles from the field; we were given radar vectors to final. At the time; ETAR was experiencing a large number of air traffic; both inbound to the field; outbound from the field; as well as training aircraft in the traffic pattern. On the downwind leg; the PM programed the course fix intercept and executed the change with the PF's verbal concurrence. The winds at ETAR were light; visibility was great; skies had a higher scattered layer; and overall it was a nice VFR day. Due to the heat rising from the surface; we did have some convective and mechanical turbulence as we approached the runway; but nothing too far out of the ordinary for a summer afternoon. The aircraft was configured on schedule (if not slightly early to be conservative) and was stable (on speed; on path; configured for landing) well before 1;000 feet. After the 'Stable' calls were made and we continued the approach; the PF disconnected automation (as briefed earlier) and continued the approach. The approach itself never destabilized as we approached the runway; however once in the flare; I noticed we were floating quite a bit. I had preloaded in my mind that floating beyond the touchdown zone would be a 'go-around' call. As we began approaching the end of the touchdown zone (and below 10 feet RA); the PM made a comment that the end of the touchdown zone was approaching. As soon as the final touchdown zone marker disappeared under the nose and before I could make the call myself; the PM called 'Go-around!' Almost as soon as this statement was made; the main tires touched down; the PF stated 'too late' and the reversers were pulled. By the time the aircraft had touched down; the flight deck had just momentarily passed the end of the touchdown zone. The aircraft decelerated normally and we cleared Runway XX on taxiway Bravo. During taxi to the terminal building; the PF stated that we would discuss the landing at the gate; to which the crew agreed. Once we were safely at the gate with the engines secured; all checklists accomplished; and the deplaning process was well underway; we had an open discussion about what had happen and why a go around should have been conducted; especially since the call was made. The debriefing was well received by the PF; who expressed that he understood why his decision to continue the landing was not correct given the circumstances. The PF mentioned that from his perspective during the landing; he believed he had still been in the touchdown zone; whereas from my perspective; we were 100-200 feet beyond it. The PF also mentioned that he saw a low altitude balked landing as being more hazardous than continuing the landing; however we reminded him that this is why bounced and balked landing recovery are part of the Extended Envelope Training received on a bi-yearly basis. After a thorough discussion and debrief; the PF stated that he agreed that we should have conducted a go-around and remained open to any suggestions/recommendations/critiques as we conducted the debrief as a crew. We discussed the no-fault go-around policy that exists and I mentioned that the response to a go-around call made by any crew member should not; and can not be 'too late;' as this breaks down CRM; places the crew in an uncomfortable situation; and establishes a scenario similar to disregarding a statement of 'I'm not comfortable with this' where one crew member's decision to continue becomes dictating (even with deteriorating safety margins) when other crew members had already expressed concern. We discussed that; although ETAR's Runway XX was long and we had ample room to decelerate after touchdown; the decision to disregard a go-around call on a shorter runway could have drastically different results. With every critique or debriefing item mentioned; the PF was incredibly receptive; understood the points being made; and was internalizing the information being discussed. I also recommended that we as a crew file reports to take advantage of a program designed for reporting slips; lapses; and errors; as well as allow each crew-member to reflect on the event in order to further analyze what could have been done differently. This event outlines a deviation from stabilized approach criteria; simply due to the landing touchdown beyond the touchdown zone. Although the correct go-around call was made; the PF continued the landing due to the immediate touchdown occurring almost immediately after the go around call was made by the PM. Expectation bias and continuation biases are incredibly powerful by influencing decision making well into the landing maneuver. Experience of landing on runways and stopping with thousands of feet remaining can cause a false sense of security when procedural drift occurs. With this event; it was incredibly beneficial to not only take time to debrief as a crew; but to see the value in each crew-member submitting an ASAP report to simply share individual perspectives from the landing. Due to the debrief; we were able to discuss company policies; procedures; as well as determine what could have been the best course of action in order to mitigate similar threats in the future. The value of a debrief can allow for crew-members to internalize constructive criticism; rather than brush off an event as an outlier. During an event such as this; I believe a tremendous amount of learning was accomplished simply by following up and discussing the events as a crew. I recommend that the normalization of debriefings after a flight; especially after flights with an event; should be standardized among the pilots. Not only are debriefings a form of mentoring tools but setting aside ego and discussing areas of possible procedural non-compliance; performance deficits; CRM breakdowns; or unmitigated threats could reinforce learning among crew-members. This ties in directly with professionalism as aviators. Although I do not believe there was an intentional violation of regulations or disregard for safety; addressing this event as a crew served to promote learning; enhance safety; and build up colleagues rather than put them down. Emphasizing the normalization of go-arounds due to unstable approaches has also been viewed as a beneficial adaptation within the Atlas culture. However; although we are seeing a statistical decrease in unstable approaches resulting in a landing; I believe this is still a work in progress as we evidently have not reached the milestone of 100% go-arounds from unstable approaches. Emphasis can also be placed in recognizing that stable approaches can still destabilize in the last 10 feet during landing; whether drifting from center-line; landing beyond the touchdown zone; or excessive pitch during the flare. This is of course in addition to ability for any operating crew member to call for a go around at any point during the approach. The go around procedure should be accomplished assoon as the call is made; and the discussion of 'why' should be reserved for when the aircraft is in level flight with checklists completed or once the aircraft is safely on the ground; parked at the gate; and checklists completed.

column121: Glide slope alert flying into RST. During approach phase of landing into RST. I was the Pilot Monitoring. We were cleared for the visual to runway 13. I recall we were descending and the Pilot Flying had the FAF altitude of 2;600 set; and the banana bar was indicating that we should level off at that altitude at approximately 0.25-0.75 prior to Ellie (FAF). I believe the approach mode was also activated; and we were fully configured. At the same time we were setting up for the approach there was a GA aircraft taking off; and a [air carrier] taxiing and holding short of Runway 13. I was focused outside and listening to ATC. We were both surprised when we received a glide slope alert. I believe that we were approximately 200 feet below the FAF when we received the alert. Around about this time I believe the 757 was cleared for takeoff. When we received the alert the Pilot Flying kicked off the autopilot; increased power and worked to re-establish a stabilized approach. We were still operating in visual conditions and had the runway in sight. From my perspective it seemed he had re-established a stabilized approach. Around 200-300 feet; or so; we received another glide slope alert. I do not recall us being excessively low to have activated the glide slope aural alert. The aircraft landed normally and safely. The Pilot Flying was frustrated that we had by all indications an unstable approach. In rethinking the events I am confident that we had the aircraft correctly configured and ready to fly the ILS with the aid of the autopilot. While we could not confirm this; our best guess was that the taxiing air carrier had somehow interfered with the glide slope and we began descending prior to the FAF. I felt that he had established a stabilized approach prior to 1;000 AGL; so the second aural alert was also a surprise. I believe it was the PF's first time flying into RST. After the first aural glide slope alert we probably should have called for a go-around. The alert caused a couple moments of confusion. We probably should have just gone around and removed the distraction.

column121: I set up for a visual approach with an ILS back-up to Runway 13L at DAL.  We were in good shape from a speed and altitude standpoint at NNATE.  I was hand flying.  I was distracted by the localizer guidance which showed me well right of course when I was actually slightly left.  I mentioned that to the Pilot Monitoring.  While troubleshooting the lateral guidance; I got behind the jet for getting flaps 15 and gear down and we ended up high.  As I regained my crosscheck; I subconsciously followed vertical guidance; which now was taking me erroneously higher. I called for flaps 30; and realized we were now 800 feet. The Pilot Monitoring called out the PAPIs (Precision Approach Indicators); which showed all white; but the glideslope guidance was taking me higher.  I called for a go-around.  My go-around was sloppy; but the Pilot Monitoring brought his A game; so it was manageable. On the second approach; a visual approach backed up by the ILS 13L; both lateral and vertical guidance were erroneous.  I had to fight the tendency to cue on the vertical guidance; which again was taking me high. The Pilot Monitoring turned off both flight directors. On short final; we got an audio glideslope alert. The glideslope raw data showed me way low. The PAPIs were three white and one red.  We should have also turned away from the ILS on the NAV tuning panel. On both approaches; an Aircraft Y was parked right at the hold short line in front of the glideslope antenna.  I suspect we will see more aircraft stopped at the hold short line with the runway closure at DAL; and so we will see more ILS interference.  I have seen similar ILS behavior at other airports with an aircraft stopped at the hold short line.  We did not write up the aircraft.  An ILS test was completed and passed prior to the flight. NOTAM (or other reminder) that the 13L ILS may be unreliable with aircraft in the ILS critical area. More training on go-arounds.
column125: An Air Carrier pilot reported they received erroneous glideslope and lateral guidance from the ILS while an aircraft was holding in the ILS critical area. The reporter stated this will become a recurring issue due to a runway closure at the airport.

column121: At GS intercept; 2000; on the ILS XX and as I was setting the missed approach altitude; 2;500; the outer ALT ring was in 1;000s increments. I intermittently set 3;000; reset 2;000; twisted outer ring to 100s increments; and set 2;500. In my haste twisting the outer ring; I unknowingly tugged the ALT select knob. We had just begun the descent on the GS. The engines went to climb power; destabilizing the approach immediately and the Captain called a go-around. I called the GA to Tower; and their instructions were runway heading to 2000. Since we were almost already at the GA altitude; the Captain stopped the go-around configuration at go-around flaps. We assumed a very quick return/rejoin was about to happen so he maintained gear down; flaps 2; and 170 kt.During our vectoring back to final with Approach Control; an ECAM was triggered simultaneously with autopilot disconnect cavalry audible alert; and the Captain said; 'TOGA LOCK?' He immediately accomplished a match/mash. ECAM showed AP (Autopilot)/ATHR (Autothrust) OFF; SLATS LOCKED and WINGTIP BRAKE. He maintained PF role as it was now raw data; with zero PFD (Primary Flight Display) information beyond airspeed; altitude and VSI. There were no characteristic speeds and no min/max symbols; Vls; Vmo; etc.; apart from the blue-selected speed caret. I ran the QRC; the QRH; and LAND APP procedures; as well as comms. The Captain elected to not declare an emergency; as Approach had broadcasted we were priority. We verified the approach was the appropriate one and reloaded it; a quick cursory brief about speeds; and we were cleared for the approach. One attempt was made to cycle the FDs for some guidance; to no avail. Autothrust would not engage. The Captain had to hand-fly the approach raw data. He successfully landed; we cleared the runway; and taxied to the gate.Slow is smooth and smooth is fast. In my haste to correct a simple mistake; I made a blunder. No fault go-around policy is great! There's no way of knowing if we'd have seen the slat lock if the first approach was flown to a landing. I had approximately XX hours on type.

column121: Landing at ID24 (short grass runway) to the North which meant there is a large hump in the middle of the runway- about 15 feet high but with a short but smooth run up to it. Asked student if I could do the landing as this was the second time I had landed on a grass strip and the first time on a runway with a pronounced hill in the middle and that hill was only about 700 feet past the threshold- I knew we would be rolling over it. If you rolled over too fast it was possible for the plane to want to become temporarily airborne. There were some very tall trees on final and it was a good idea to touch down as close to the threshold as possible. The idea was to fly a very stabilized approach as low as possible over the trees and then get into the flare just as we were crossing the shore of the lake where the runway began. Set up a long straight-in over Garfield Bay; crossed the shore when I realized I did not recognize the landmarks at the lower altitude we were at; and was already descending to what I thought were the tops of the trees on final.  I think we were below the legal altitude limit a few times when considering how far from the airport we were so I climbed a little so as not to bother residents and get a better view.  Then my student who knows the area well spotted the landmarks I needed. Set up the steep approach and the rest went better than expected. Contributing factors were [the] challenging approach and landing in the type of plane we were flying had me anxious- would have been really easy in a Aircraft X. More challenging to me than landing at Telluride with a 25 gusting 35 crosswind during light snow (Aircraft Y). I did not pick out my landmarks for the approach with the idea I could not orient so well when down low. Did not do a low approach to check out the runway where I wouldn't be so worried about getting stabilized on the approach early- we just overflew the airport at pattern altitude having taken off from Timber Basin about 40 minutes earlier. Did not just do a left downwind/base/final because I was worried about being super-stabilized for the short/soft field landing.  I could have just done a longer downwind and my orientation would have been very good. The high terrain on the left downwind had something to do with my decision. How to avoid [is to] do a better job of picking out landmarks for making an approach plan to challenging airports. Fly the pattern as long as it is safe. At short unprepared strips with tall trees all around it do a low approach and check things out; ensure safety and make a good plan.

column121: FO Flying / AP on in Nav mode. Just as we were making our turn to transition from the arrival to the approach. FO selected APP mode on FCU the LOC & GS  captured simultaneously . Initially the GS indicated below us but as soon as the aircraft went to capture mode the GS indication jumped above the 1/2 scale deflection up. The aircraft followed the GS as it moved up to 1/2 scale high until the aircraft pitch was approximately 20 degrees nose up. Thrust had been at idle so the speed vector showed a negative trend while the engines were trying to accelerate.  The power finally caught up and the nose came back down . We were able to continue the approach and land. I talked to the crew from the day before and they had a similar thing happen to them.  There is an airport very close to this intersection. I wonder if there maybe some interference from the [other] airport. If there is some interference we need to add a note to the 10-7 pages to warn the crews this situation could occur.

column121: On descent into ZZZ; we set up for the visual to XX backed up by the ILS. I was the pilot flying. We were given a runway change from Runway XX to XY. We made the necessary changes and briefed them. The ILS frequency for XX and XY at ZZZ are the same. After we were not receiving a good signal on approach to XY; we asked Approach if they had switched from XX to XY. He responded roger and sent us to Tower. He sounded busy and both the Captain and I agreed that he had not really processed the question.Once with Tower; we asked the same thing and they advised us to let them know if we ever got a good signal while on approach. We didn't have good ILS guidance and the Captain suggested I click off the autopilot and hand-fly it down. I did so and was working to maneuver myself on final. I overshot final while continuing to descend. I was worried that this would trigger a GPWS warning as I could see that I was near high terrain to the south of the field. I adjusted my vertical speed as I corrected toward final; but still got a GPWS warning. I responded to the warning as I continued correcting back to runway centerline.We were immediately out of the GPWS threat and elected to continue the visual approach but we were left high after the response. It was here that I should have seen we were not in a good position to continue and should have gone around. As we continued down final; we were at idle thrust and flight spoilers were out as we continued to configure the aircraft. I don't believe that we were fully configured below 1000 ft.; which was outside of stabilized approach criteria; and we still had the flight spoilers extended - which; after review; was against company limitation. By 500 ft. we were fully configured but still fast and slightly high. Another opportunity to conduct a go-around. The power remained in idle for almost the entire approach. We landed on Runway XY.Due to poor maneuvering to final while on visual approach; an overshooting final resulted in a terrain warning. Rather than make the decision to terminate the approach there; we continued; which resulted in an unstabilized approach that continued to a landing. [The cause was also] failure to call a go-around when that was the appropriate course of action. When you think you should go around; go around. Don't wait for someone else to call it and don't continue an approach that isn't stable. Follow SOP and your training.
column123: GPWS warning followed by an unstable approach into Runway XY in ZZZ. We were descending into ZZZ and were set up for Runway XX. Approach advised that winds had shifted and asked if we would like Runway XY. We accepted and began to set up for XY. When we had the airport in sight; we were cleared for the visual. As we armed the approach for the autopilot; we were told by Approach that the localizer and glideslope may not be working. My First Officer turned off the autopilot and began hand-flying the visual. He became distracted by configuring the airplane and was flying through the localizer when I informed him he needed to start his turn. As he began the turn he started descending; which caused a GPWS warning for a couple seconds due to terrain. We were confident of terrain clearance but leveled off until we were back on the localizer. Once we were on the localizer; we were high. We began our descent and continued configuring. We weren't fully configured until about 800 ft. AGL. We proceeded with the landing without further event.I think fatigue played a factor in our poor decision-making to continue the approach. We had a [before-dawn] wake-up call that morning and this was our 4th leg of the day. We had been dealing with thunderstorms and delays all day long.

column118: Flight Crew Took Evasive Action
column121: ATC slowed us to 210; 190; then 170 KIAS; and then vectored us at 4;000 feet on a base leg for the visual approach to [Runway] 34L. As we rolled out on our base leg vector heading; a B737 was 3 miles ahead; at our 11 o' clock and about 1;000 feet above. ATC had us follow that traffic; cleared visual approach 34L. In the flight deck; we discussed the options to increase our separation; and reduce wake turbulence; behind the preceding aircraft. We decided to make the turn as square as possible without blowing through the localizer (visual approaches were also being conducted on the parallel Runway 34R); and stay one dot high on the glide path. We made a square turn to intercept the localizer; but the aircraft was still only about three miles ahead. As we began our descent below 4;000 feet; we encountered wake turbulence. Significant control inputs were required to correct for the resulting 'Dutch Roll' motion (wing lateral roll/rocking motion coupled with tail wag directional motion). The turbulence lasted for approximately 15-20 seconds; as we raised our glide path to about one dot above; which allowed us to exit the wake turbulence by about 3;000 feet. We maintained a higher glide path for the rest of the approach; keeping us clear of the turbulence for the rest of the visual approach to landing. Although the aircraft remained under positive control throughout the turbulence encounter; and within stable visual approach parameters for the entire approach; the motion was very uncomfortable and noticeable to everyone on board. Checking with our flight attendants after the event; no one in the cabin was injured; but a lot of passengers were understandably giving the flight attendants weird looks. Both flight attendants had recognized the motion as wake turbulence as it was occurring.A busy airport can present challenges with aircraft separation and wake turbulence avoidance; especially on visual approaches. ATC requested a specific speed for sequencing; presumably to fit us in behind the B737 and in front of other arrivals. Under other circumstances where the preceding aircraft is at the same altitude or lower; three miles of spacing behind a B737 may not have resulted in wake turbulence. It is a larger aircraft but not a heavy. However; in this case; the higher altitude of the preceding aircraft guaranteed that we would start out below its glide path; at least until we could cross its glide path to become established on a higher glide path. Therefore; the spacing really was not adequate for wake turbulence avoidance; since additional time should have been allowed for the vortices to dissipate prior to our crossing of the preceding aircraft's glide path (and those vortices). We suspect ATC was not accounting for the higher altitude of the preceding aircraft when they stacked us in so close behind the preceding aircraft. In the future; we will be more assertive and ask ATC for a lower speed; or for delay vectors; prior to the visual approach; even if that means ATC has to work harder to fit us in somewhere else in the sequence.

column121: We were making a visual approach to Runway XXR in ZZZ. The airport was is sight; while the downwind leg and convective activity was present south and west of the airport. After receiving landing clearance from Tower; a wind check was issued and landing speeds adjusted accordingly. The First Officer (FO) was flying and called for landing Flaps 30. The Before Landing Checklist was accomplished and I did a second wind check. We discussed the new speeds and continued the approach. Shortly thereafter; we received a Too Low Flaps Warning. Upon inspection I discovered that the flaps were in the 25 detention. The flaps were moved to position 30. I considered a go-around; but elected to continue the approach as we were on speed and path and a go-around would have been into the convective activity.Obviously; this was a failure to read and verify the checklist items. While debriefing the event with the FO; we both agreed that the monitoring of the wind/ weather was a factor as we considered our performance on final. Also being in a MAX 8 was a factor as the location of the flap gauge is different than the NG; possibly affecting the visual flow of the checklist items. Other factors were the weather deviations on arrival and non-standard arrival into a visual from the north side of the airport and terrain monitoring. Passenger issues requiring Dispatch coordination and the aircraft being met by Law Enforcement at the gate. It was a very busy flight at the end of a long day.
column125: B737 MAX8 Captain cited fleet differences; and checklist compliance contributing to a Too Low Flaps warning during a visual approach with convective weather also being a distraction.  Procedural deviation was corrected; stability evaluated and approach and landing completed.

column121: I flew a night operation into PTKK.  While I was aware of the NOTAM'd PAPI being out of service; and the flight was flown in accordance with SOP; I would like to make the [company] aware of the potential risk associated when conducting a night  RNAV(GPS) 4 PTKK approach under these conditions.  I believe this reduction in safety at night is due to several factors:1) Lack of visual reference on short final (black hole effect).2) RNAV(GPS) 4 approach lacks vertical guidance to the runway; resulting in the FD's initiating a missed approach at HAMAX.3) Reaching the MAP 300 ft. higher than published DDA.  If not briefed and aware; the FD will command a left turn and level flight prior to the 'expected' minimums.4) Lack of outside and inside references to determine if you meet the stabilized approach criteria once you are in visual conditions.5) PTKK is one of shortest runways in our system.6) Numerous threats associated with preloading an electronic glide path while still on the published approach; especially in IMC conditions (a technique many use to increase the level of safety) 7) Junior and less experienced pilots typically fly this trip.

column121: It was my second day on the line. This was a repositioning  flight with two other crew members in the back who I was taking to ZZZ to fly their lines. I was on standby for the first half of the day and I had just flown ZZZ to ZZZ1 then to ZZZ2 and was in IMC from take off to landing on each flight. I was unaware upon leaving ZZZ1 that they had scheduled me to fly these other pilots to ZZZ after reaching ZZZ2. So I operated the flight and all went according to plan except I had an unstable approach into ZZZ. Using the auto pilot for the approach I ended up descending below minimum altitude for the final approach fix and continued well below glide slope until I was approximately 1200 AGL at which point I disconnected autopilot; increased power to help arrest the descent rate; caught up to the glide slope and hand flew the rest of the approach. I relied too heavily on the autopilot and lost situational awareness. I also could have asked one of the other pilots to act as copilot.I think it would be safer to not have new hire Captains fly without an FO (First Officer) in hard IMC for multiple legs even if it is just a reposition flight. And if there is a crew member on board they should sit right seat.

Stopped on taxiway for inspection by fire personnel; nothing noted; taxi to gate."

column121: En route from ZZZ1 to ZZZ2 we were directed by the physician on call to divert to ZZZ which we had just passed at FL350. The Captain gave me controls of the aircraft and radios and began the fix strategy. We were given a heading direct to ZZZ and began a descent to the airport. We began the approach on a visual right downwind as there was a cell to the north and west of the airport we wanted to avoid. Selected speed was chosen as we slowed down; however; when managed speed was selected the aircraft accelerated because I had not activated the approach. We captured the error; however; these few seconds caused us to be high and fast. Given the already high-altitude airport and compressed diversion time for the medical issue; we elected to do a visual left 360 and rejoin final. We had selected the final approach fix altitude; however; when we did our left 360 turn it took us away from the airport. The Tower Controller alerted us that he had an altitude alert and we climbed back to the 7;200 feet altitude for the fix we were now joining the ILS. We continued the approach without further issue.Cause - 1. Compressed; high pressure with the medical emergency of passenger with 60/40 blood pressure.2. Out of sequence normal approach and landing with the diversion that greatly compressed our time from top of descent to landing. 3. Choosing ZZZ - high altitude/high terrain as a divert airport.4. Loss of situational awareness as we made the turn away from the airport.Suggestions -1. Take vectors out for a longer final approach.2. If possible; choose diversion airports that have less threats. 3. If a turn for an airport is needed to descend or slow - make them towards the airport not away as altitude is less likely going to be an issue with regards to terrain avoidance.
column123: During a medical divert we were high and fast.  On approach we accomplished a left 360 to descend.   We initially had 7;300 feet set and when we turned inbound and had the field insight we began a descent to 6;300 feet the final approach fix altitude.  Approach received a low altitude alert and informed us.  We climbed and continued the approach visually.  We never descended below 6;300 feet before the FAF.Visual approach; high and fast resulted in loss of situational awareness.  Avoid visual approaches when in unplanned situations; get vectors for an instrument approach.

column121: I was the Pilot Flying on the ZZZZZ 1 Arrival into ZZZ. ZZZ TRACON asked us what approach we wanted to [Runway] XXR into ZZZ. We requested the RNAV visual to [Runway] XXR. We were cleared to 7;000 feet and cleared to intercept the final approach course for [Runway] XXR after ZZZZZ. ZZZ TRACON informed us of a Low Altitude Alert and had us climb to 7;000 feet; then he Immediately cleared us for the visual. Told us to take down the phone number for possible pilot deviation.
column123: Both our first time to ZZZ.  We were cleared to intercept final segment. We said to each other; 'ZZZZZ is IAF;' and then we determined it was the final course we were supposed to intercept.  We should have asked ATC to repeat; but we put 4;000 feet in the autopilot; as in VNAV; to continue published approach; but 7;000 feet was still our clearance limit.  On the published segment it continued down; inside ZZZZZ; below 7;000 feet.  ATC queried us at 6;000 feet and told us to climb.  We had the field in sight and ATC then cleared us immediately for the visual.I was so focused on terrain and being in VNAV. As Pilot Monitoring; I should have questioned the clearance and any confusion we had. I am sure what we heard; thought we heard and what ATC said were all different.

column121: Early descent and low speed condition resulting in go-around. I was the Pilot Flying. Very short flight. Visual approach to RWY 15 at CHS. I was expecting the ILS approach. I missed that the GS for RWY 15 was out of service. We had briefed the OSPRI7 STAR. ATC recleared us direct to the airport and eventually direct MAMAE intersection to join the localizer for [Runway] 15 at 3;000 [feet] ft. Airport was in sight and we were cleared for a visual approach. I armed the approach and when we went into altitude capture at 3;000 [feet] I incorrectly set an altitude below 3;000 [feet] to allow the descent to continue in order to join the glide slope. I then realized we were not receiving the glide slope and were below the FAF crossing altitude at 1000ft. I disconnected the autopilot and stopped the descent. Unfortunately I did not also call for the flight directors off initially. I am relatively new to the equipment. Since we were in landing configuration of course the speed dropped rapidly. We were in landing configuration with the airport and PAPI's in sight but since the approach was unstable I decided to go around. We then flew the RWY 15 RNAV (GPS)Y to a normal landing. It was the third leg of a busy day with thunderstorm avoidance on the first two flights. It is my practice to carefully review the NOTAMs but somehow I missed the GS OTS. I haven't been into CHS as yet and the flight is very short and our ATC clearance had us high from the top of descent.Direct to a new airport on a time compressed flight for a visual at night is never in my opinion a pleasant way to conclude a long day of flying. Upon honest reflection I fully acknowledge I had a strong expectation bias for the ILS to 15. The lack of the GS and the unexpected consequences placed us in a reactionary position which is unacceptable.Our training is clear on how to capture and correct erroneous situations should they occur and we did our best to stop and correct an inadvertently entered unstable position. This was a clear example of the value and necessity of our human factors training. I know these things have happened before and I consider it my professional responsibility to not allow it to happen to me. And yet it did. Recognizing; and fully appreciating how higher than expected workload situations can present themselves is an essential aspect to our work. An increased diligence regarding the review of NOTAMs is a resounding suggestion I'm making to myself for one. Also; the need for particularly increased vigilance when in situations such as this is something I'll never forget.

column121: On a flight landing in EWR; the ATIS advertised the Stadium Visual to Runway 29.  Although the environment was busy due to the airspace congestion; everything was working out smoothly and well.  Approach mentioned to us a few different aircraft along the way to use caution for due to wake turbulence and we did use caution for the several large aircraft we saw during the terminal phase of the approach.  I do not recall; however; my Pilot Monitoring later said the Tower upon clearing us to land issued another caution for wake turbulence for a 747.  I did not see any flying aircraft that would pose any wake turbulence threat.  The approach was normal and I was right on my target speeds.  I closed both throttles all the way during the flare; and both engines settled all the way to idle.  The tires were maybe an inch or two from the surface; the yoke back bumping the stop; all the flying air gone from the wings; touchdown imminent; [when] a large gust from the left shoved the aircraft aggressively to the right.  The gust raised the left wing up and also the whole aircraft up into the air several feet. When the gust reached the tail; it shoved the vertical stabilizer right moving the nose left.  The centerline of the runway went from centered on the nose gear to well outside the main gear near the left wing tip.  There were two gusts one right after the other; both the same and having the same effect on the aircraft.  I immediately shoved both throttles all the way forward and countered with the controls to maintain orientation.  I don't recall calling the go-around; but my Pilot Monitoring says I called it right away. The air was smooth after the two gusts; the plane settled some towards the ground as it gained momentum.  Once everything was straightened out and the airplane with some flying speed now climbing from the runway instead of closing toward it; I realized my right arm was still holding the throttles against the stop.  Knowing that could be very bad for the engines; I looked inside to see how badly they were over revving and simultaneously pulled them back off the stop some.  I never got the chance to see because I noticed the left engine sitting at idle while the right engine was making full power.  That's when I realized my right leg was holding about 80% right rudder travel in.  The gear was up and the flaps sitting at 15 degrees and we were making only 125 knots.  Then after several more seconds; the left engine began to slowly make its way through the 50%'s; 60s; 70s; 80s; and finally into the low 90% of N1 speed.  With everything back to normal; we cleaned up the aircraft and settled in for the next approach.  ATC again offered us the Stadium Visual to 29 but said the ILS 22R was available.  We took the ILS 22R.  ATC never asked us why we went around.  We did not make any communications to our team in the cabin until after shutdown.  The origin of the gust was immediately very clear to both of us in the cockpit.  It was jet blast from jet engines pointed at our flight path during our touchdown; departing aircraft on 22R beginning their takeoff roll were blasting the landing zone of 29 with their engines.  Perhaps the cautionary statements we received from ATC for wake turbulence were referring to jet blast from airlines beginning to roll on the ground and not from wake turbulence from aircraft in flight.  Had I been aware this was the case; I would have never accepted 29 as a landing option.  The 22R & L runways cross Runway 29; so I envisioned aircraft departing across our landing path as they took off on the 22s.  So I would see them and be aware of the timing for jet blast and wake from landing aircraft.  However; instead; airlines were making intersection departures after the 29 intersection; shortening their takeoff distance.  So instead of crossing our path; they were starting from our left and starting their takeoff roll; and moving away to our left.  I never looked left during my landing; so I don'tknow what was there blasting us; I'm guessing something big.  Of note; due to high fuel price in EWR; we were heavy; just a few hundred pounds under max landing weight.  I would first suggest not landing small jets on [Runway] 29 at EWR when 22s are departing in the way they were.  I wasn't familiar enough with EWR to know how dangerous the circumstance would be; I've landed on 22s in years past.  I would also suggest the left engine acceleration be repaired to an acceptable level.  Waiting 15 - 20 seconds for an engine to give power in fight is not acceptable.  If neither engine had given power for that amount of time; I do not believe a touchdown without major damage would've been possible.  Even still; it took everything the aircraft had in it plus everything humanly possible I could do to prevent an accident.

column121: Captain [was] Pilot Flying. He briefed the OSPRI arrival and ILS to [Runway] 15. ATC kept us high; then Cleared for the straight in visual. Gear; flaps and speed brakes out to get down. We both missed that the glideslope was out of service on the ATIS. We realized we were too low and leveled off around 1;000 feet. At some point we got the low energy speed warning. We had left the speed brakes out. Retracted them. Disconnected the FD's. Captain called for a go around. Preformed the GA and returned for the RNAV 15. Busy day flying around storms. Last flight at night. Short flight. ATC keeping us high. Expectation bias to the ILS. I had recently been to CHS at night and the ILS was working. New Captain to [aircraft]; all his other time was in Boeing aircraft. My failure to trap our errors earlier. I will create more effective barriers going forward. For example: Request ILS/RNAV approaches vs visual at night. Get delay vectors when ATC keeps us high.

column121: On the final phase of the Visual Approach; backed up by the ILS Runway XY to ZZZ; with the intention to capture the glide path from above; the Auto Pilot (AP) and Auto Throttles were disengaged. Prevailing conditions were Good Visibility and varying Tail Winds of up to nine (9) Knots. During the final portion of the descent to touchdown; with a visual transition to the PAPI reference already established (PAPI were showing 3 RED-1 WHITE); the Aircraft descended below the referenced visual glide path; with the tendency to fly over the Runway XY overrun area at an altitude (AGL) of less than 50 ft. The moment the Captain; having the Pilot Monitoring (PM) duties; mentioned his estimation for being low on the glide path; the aircraft was overflying a point short of the RWY by about 500 feet. He consecutively called for a Go Around; which was immediately executed by the PF (Pilot Flying). The Flight landed after a RADAR pattern on the same runway uneventfully.[The cause was] Human Factors / Pilot Flying error on visually estimating the projected Glide Slope to Runway XY; with the current Tail Wind conditions. Root cause is the initially higher angle of descent / rate of descent to capture the glide slope from above; that ended up to an over correction - reverse situation of flying finally lower than the projected/correct slope. [I suggest] earlier Aircraft configuration for the Final Approach Phase and timely request from the ATC for lower altitudes / earlier descent to the recommended altitudes that lead to a better projected sequence of flying tasks.
column123: FO was flying pilot. Performed visual approach and FO had a hard time trying to catch the glide slope from above because he was trying to slow down and put flaps down while on OPEN DESC. FO called for 'the bird'; which caught me off guard because it is the first time anyone has asked for the flight path vector while trying to intercept the ILS and while disconnecting the AP. He not only disconnected AP but also eventually the auto thrust without previously briefing it. I told him to never disconnect the auto thrust again while trying to save a visual approach. Finally he was established on the ILS. Below 500 feet he said he was going to follow the PAPI. I glanced down at the VSI to make sure we were stable and when I looked backed up we had 4 red on the PAPIs and at the same time we received a GS GPWS warning. Became unstable. I loudly and sternly called 'unstable; go-around.' Performed go-around as RA called 50 feet. FO performed a soft go-around. I overrode his decision after he brought back TL (Thrust Lever) to CLB and I pushed TL to TOGA since we were in ZZZ (high airport elevation) with high OAT. I honestly could not tell you the altitude when the FO brought back the TL to CLB. I wanted to make sure we were safe since the go-around was performed at 50 feet from an unsafe position. At 50 feet RA; aircraft was not anywhere near the approach end of the runway. Came back for an uneventful landing.FO descended below glide slope and PAPI showed 4 red.Soft Go-around procedure should be changed or at least highlight the need to take into account airport elevation and OAT when deciding to make a soft go-around from a low altitude with an obvious performance deterioration.Soft Go-around procedure should be changed or at least highlight the need to take into account airport elevation and OAT when deciding to make a soft go-around from a low altitude with an obvious performance deterioration.

column121: Upon configuring flaps from 25 to 30; Trailing Edge Flaps indicated a split and stopped tracking at 27 degrees. LE FLAPS TRANSIT light was also illuminated (amber light). A go-around was accomplished to enable time for non normal checklists to be run. LEADING EDGE FLAPS TRANSIT checklist was run which led us into TRAILING EDGE FLAPS DISAGREE checklist. Flaps were left at 27 degrees with flap handle at 25 setting. Vref15 +5 was the approach speed selected and an uneventful landing and taxi to the gate was accomplished.
column123: On Final Approach to XXL in ZZZ and configuring from flaps 25 to flaps 30 at about 1;500 feet the trailing edge flap needle indicators displayed a split and the flaps stopped at approximately 27 degrees. The LE FLAP TRANSIT light was also illuminated. After taking a few seconds to realize what was happening and coming through approximately 1;000 ft the Captain called for a go around. I executed the go around; however upon attempting to retract the flaps we realized they were stuck at 27 degrees and would not retract at all. I continued to fly the plane and the Captain advised ATC and then ran the non-normal LEADING EDGE FLAP TRANSIT QRH followed by the TRAILING EDGE FLAP DISAGREE QRH. We received vectors from ATC and took our time setting up for a flaps 27 landing using the VREF15+5 knot wind additive. While being vectored back to the final approach course the Captain TEST and the FA's and made an announcement to the PAX. We landed and taxied to the gate uneventfully.

column121: I took off from ZZZ in my 2008 CAW SportCruiser; an aircraft that I had recently purchased. This was my first flight from ZZZ where I had based the aircraft. I flew around the local area to familiarize myself with landmarks in a 15 NM area near ZZZ. When I returned to land; an aircraft that was returning to the airport announced their intention to land on runway XX. I set up to enter the pattern for that runway about 5 NM away and went over the approach in my mind. Then; when I was about 3 NM away; I heard a pilot announce he was entering a left downwind for runway XY. I was surprised and concerned and made changes to my power setting and flaps setting from downwind settings. I changed my approach to enter a crosswind for runway XY. I was still a little shaken by the sudden change in approach for landing. The downwind and base legs were uneventful. The final approach started well; but as I neared the runway threshold; the PAPI system indicated that I was below the glide slope. I added a little power to get back on the glide slope; but I was too far into the landing. The aircraft hit hard and then the nose gear collapsed. The aircraft slid about 250 feet on top of the nose gear fairing and cowling. The prop struck the asphalt and one blade snapped off. Fortunately; I was thoroughly strapped into the four-point restraint harness and was uninjured.In retrospect; I should have abandoned the approach when it became unstable; and initiated a go-around. This will be a very expensive lesson. I am grateful that I emerged from the experience physically unscathed.

column121: Close call event on short final to Runway 13 and unstable approach at ZZZ after finishing a long cross-country home from ZZZ1. Event occurred after being cleared for the visual approach to Runway 13 from Approach and switching to the local advisory frequency. I made a call on the advisory frequency on a 5 mile initial to the left downwind for Runway 31; intending to enter the traffic pattern. The aircraft was descending and I was very fast; 130 KIAS; with the intent to slow the aircraft in the pattern. Switching back to Approach frequency to cancel the IFR flight plan I decided on a straight in approach to Runway 13 to save time; while on Approach I missed the local traffic call of an aircraft taking Runway 31 to depart. After switching back to the local advisory frequency I made an advisory call for a 3 mile final to Runway 13 and I was switching the landing runway. The ADS-B data link immediately showed a traffic target directly ahead and climbing from Runway 31; passengers in my airplane spotted the traffic; and the other aircraft and I communicated to ensure we would avoid each other. Being distracted by the traffic close call; handling the radio swaps; and changing our approach I did not slow the aircraft down or add full flaps. Attempted to land with two notches of flaps and very fast; floated the aircraft most of the runway with braking in the final 1000 ft. Factors and Chain of Events:1. Long day of flying; and being tired from week at ZZZ1 contributed to wanting to be home. Additional time pressure of needing to pick up the family dog from the boarder affected decision making on speeds to fly and ultimately the runway selection.2. Concerns about forgetting to close the IFR flight plan; especially when tired; under time pressures; and distractions of passengers (kids); etc; contributed to cancelling in the air very close to the local airport operations. I should have stayed on the advisory frequency whenever within 5 miles of the airport that is known to be busy. Without ADS-B I would not have known about the departing traffic.3. Long two weeks of very good; challenging; and very safe flying supported an overconfidence in flying the airplane outside of SOPs; which contributed to flying way too fast on short final and almost floating the entire runway. Established patterns; speeds; altitudes; and power settings are practiced and used for good reason...to take variability out of flying and allow for handling of non-routine issues. Flying outside of SOP adds variability and multiplies risks that could overwhelm the pilot.4. Inaction of sticking with the originally stated plan to enter the downwind for Runway 31; the radio call is not just an advisory of what I am doing; but provides information to other operators on how the airport is flowing. Changing the plan adds risk to all of the other operators.

column121: Visual approaches to Runway XXR were in use at ZZZ at the time. Due to heavy traffic in the vicinity of the airport; and terrain to the southwest; ATC kept us on a vector that aligned us with a roughly 2 mile final approach to XXR. The airport was extremely difficult to obtain visually; and neither the FO nor myself had it in sight until about 4 miles out. We called the airport in sight; and were cleared for visual approach. At that time; I turned the aircraft to the right to align the aircraft for a downwind in a left hand traffic pattern; and descended the aircraft to 6;500 feet; the appropriate altitude for the 5 mile visual fix. Reaching 6;500 feet; we began to feel uneasy; as the terrain south of the airport forms a bowl; with terrain reaching 8;000 feet in places. Once at the 6;500 feet altitude; we could visually see that there wasn't any room to maneuver the aircraft in the valley to align it with the final approach course (we had about a 3 mile wide; terrain-free swath to work with). At about the same time; ATC issued us a low altitude warning; with the accompanying 'check altitude immediately' instruction. At that time; while on a heading corresponding with base leg; we executed a climb to 7;500 feet; and told tower we were aborting the approach. We overflew the airport and executed a right hand turn to set up for right traffic for XXR; a visual approach which was completed successfully. [Cause was] ATC's vector that led us into a valley not wide enough to maneuver the aircraft on approach. Poor charting of terrain on VFR maps in company publications. I'm filling out a separate safety concern report recommending an additional alert page on the company charts advising pilots of the difficulty of shooting a close-in visual approach to runway XXR from the West.
column123: ZZZ was conducting visual approaches to runways XXR and XXL at the time of the event. ZZZ Approach was descending us for arrival and told us to expect the visual approach for Runway XXR. At about 20 miles from the airport; we were told to proceed direct to the airport and report the field in sight for the visual. At this time we were also told to maintain 9;000 feet for terrain and traffic in the area. We did not obtain visual contact with the airport until about 5 miles away; at which point we informed ZZZ Approach and they cleared us for the visual and told us to contact the tower. At this point the approach controller has us on a vector to intercept approximately 2 mile final for Runway XXR. The Captain made a turn to the right and began a descent to traffic pattern altitude of 6;500 feet to maneuver the aircraft for a stable approach and landing. This turn brought us toward gradually rising terrain northeast of the airport. As we continued to maneuver the aircraft; it became apparent that there was not enough room to safely align with the runway. We initiated a climb and turned back toward the airport. At this time tower issued a low altitude warning and told us to 'check altitude immediately' (we were climbing through 7;000 feet at this time). We responded and said that we were climbing and turning back toward to the airport and that we would need to abort the approach and turn right to re-enter right traffic for XXR. A safe approach and landing was then completed without further incident.ZZZ Approach kept us high due to terrain and traffic and issued a tight vector for a visual approach that only allowed for a 2 mile final for the landing runway. Contributing factors included a busy GA traffic environment and inability to see the airport visually until about 5 miles away.A better way to conduct this approach when arriving from the west would be to join the RNAV approach from vectors or an initial approach fix (especially if this were to be conducted at night) or to vector the aircraft over the airport and down the valley to enter a right downwind for XXR.

column121: I was First Officer (FO) on Aircraft X operating to ZZZ. We were conducting the ILS XXR circle to XY approach when we received an abnormal landing gear indication. When I lowered the landing gear; the annunciator panel showed 3 red lights along with 3 green lights. The overhead panel showed 3 green as well. We briefly discussed continuing the landing as there was some confidence the gear was down. The discussion continued until around 500 ft. and I felt strongly that we shouldn't be making such consequential decisions that close to the ground without running a checklist. We performed a go-around; ran the checklist; got the gear down without the red lights; and landed successfully.
column123: ILS XXR circle XY at ZZZ. Lowered gear just prior to FAF (Final Approach Fix). 3 red; 3 green illuminated. After evaluating with First Officer (FO); executed go-around on XY due to gear disagree. Standard missed. Ran checklists. Vectored for longest runway... ILS XXR. Extended gear on 10-mile base. All indications normal. Executed normal landing. Taxi to gate to find puddle [of] hydraulic fluid at left main. Possibly unrelated.

column121: I was the Pilot Flying. The weather in ZZZ was deteriorating quickly. Thunderstorms; heavy rain and wind were moving towards the airport. On downwind we could see the airport through the spotty rain showers and were number 3 for the RNAV XX approach. The previous two aircraft reported light turbulence; heavy rain; negative windshear; and they broke out right at minimums. The weather radar depicted a large cell just to the south of the airport moving in quickly and one at the very end of the runway moving towards the airport. Lightning was also depicted on the weather radar. We broke out right at minimums with heavy rain on short final however the runway was visible and I continued with the remainder of the approach visually. At about 300 ft. I encountered a wind gust that caused the aircraft to go above the VASI glidepath slightly. I recovered by lowering the nose of the aircraft and also being cognizant of the increase descent rate that would occur. Even though there was heavy rain; the runway was still visible. With the heavy rain and wipers on max rate; it was difficult to hear anything else. My First Officer informed me that he heard an alert through the aircraft speakers that sounded like 'Don't Sink' but it could have been 'Sink Rate.' He wasn't sure. I glanced at the VSI and it was indicating about a 1;200 fpm descent rate. Speed was about 10 kts. Fast. I arrested our descent rate and got back on the VASI glidepath. I removed my headset from my ear to hear any further aural warnings. At about 100 ft. with a VSI of 850 FPM I also heard 'Don't Sink' in conjunction with the 100 ft. aural call. The descent rate had been stabilized and it was evident that a safe landing could be made within the Touchdown Zone. We were both quite certain there was no question that a safe landing could be made and also; if we initiated a go-around we would have gone directly into the thunderstorm at the end of the runway. I decided that the safest course of action at that given time was to land. I elected to land the aircraft rather than follow the Company requirement of initiating a go-around when a 'Don't Sink' Alert was received. The landing was uneventful. We touched down in the Landing Zone as briefed. Deceleration was smooth and as we were taxiing towards the gate the ensuing thunderstorms enveloped the field but the wait for our gate was not that long. We arrived at the gate a bit behind schedule. Company policy is quite clear on what is expected and required when encountering this; or any other EGPWS Alerts. I think our situation was a little precarious due to the weather; heavy rain; wipers and also a lack of direct communication as to what was heard. A prevention in this situation would be to not be in this situation. Hold; wait out the storm. Divert as necessary. Company aircraft send Aural Warnings through the speakers. There are some aircraft where the speaker volume is not that loud. Better planning and briefing would also be a factor in preventing this from happening.
column123: I was Pilot Monitoring during RNAV XX Approach to ZZZ. Last weather report from tower was 10 miles visibility and winds 150 degrees at 23 kts. gusting to 28 kts. with no reported wind shear however it was evident visually as well as on radar that a thunderstorm was rapidly approaching the field from the south.  Because of this we were vectored north of the field and cleared the approach. We discussed the published missed approach would take us toward the incoming storm and how to mitigate the risk if we had to go missed approach. At the DA; the runway was in sight (despite a quick to develop rain shower between the FAF and DA) and would remain so throughout the landing; thus we continued the approach. However; on the descent from the DA; the gusty conditions resulted in the aircraft being above glideslope and in an effort to correct glidepath; the aural 'Sink Rate' sounded on short final; followed by a sink rate call from me. The PF (Pilot Flying) made appropriate corrections; maintaining a stable airspeed despite the gusty conditions; and landed in the Touchdown Zone without incident. The post flight report noted a max sink rate of 1;215 fpm. Although the sink rate aural caution sounded; I feel the safest action was to continue the landing considering the runway remained in sight from the DA to touchdown; airspeed remained stable and a go-around would result in flying the aircraft toward the fast moving thunderstorm enveloping the airfield.

column121: We were vectored for the Visual approach 13R ZZZ.  (ILS Out of service).  We were cleared for the visual approach a few miles NE of ZZZZZ.  The aircraft was slowly descending through 2;200 ft. when we received a GPWS alert (obstacle alert) for a tower 1605 feet between us and ZZZZZ.  We immediately climbed back to 2800 feet. (Glide slope verification altitude). Climbing back to 2800 ft through ZZZZZ put us high on the approach. We configured quickly and ran checklist. Through 1000 feet we were still high on VASI but agreed must be stable by 500 in visual conditions. I realized through 800 feet we had not contacted the tower; tried to contact tower twice and realized we had the incorrect frequency; finally contacted tower passing 400 ft and was cleared to land.  Normal landing.The ILS was out of service. The pilot flying was new to the airbus (3 months) and not totally confident yet with the airplane. I let myself get a little complacent;  the weather was clear and I had flown many legs in the last 7  days. As pilot monitoring; I should have seen that we were drifting low outside ZZZZZ.  After getting high on the approach; should have requested vectors back around for a new approach to give us more time to reorganize.
